EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 170 kt upper level jet dove south out of British Columbia into Washington and Oregon by early Saturday November 16th which provided strong lift and precipitation across north-central Idaho into western Montana. An arctic cold front brought gusty winds and heavy snow at times across Glacier National park and along US-2 from West Glacier to Marias Pass on the 16th.  As the orientation of the upper level jet became more northwesterly by the 17th and moved off to the north and east, showers were felt across much of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Glacier Park International Airport received 3 of total storm snowfall that mainly fell on November 17th. The temperatures on the morning of the 17th ranged from the upper 20s to near 30 degrees F. and road conditions were slick due to the snow in the Kalispell area. Other measured storm snowfall totals include: 3 Columbia Falls, 2.5 8 mi. north of Finley Point, 2 3 mi. east-northeast of Bigfork, 2 5 mi. north of Creston, and 1 4 mi. east-southeast Pablo.

In addition, flooding of small streams and creeks occurred across the area along with debris flows on the Ash Creek burn scar area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 170 kt upper level jet dove south out of British Columbia into Washington and Oregon by early Saturday November 16th which provided strong lift and precipitation across north-central Idaho into western Montana. An arctic cold front brought gusty winds and heavy snow at times across Glacier National park and along US-2 from West Glacier to Marias Pass on the 16th.  As the orientation of the upper level jet became more northwesterly by the 17th and moved off to the north and east, showers were felt across much of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS employee reported that 2 inches of snow had already fallen on the road at mile marker 4 on Interstate 90 by 4:54 pm MST on Nov. 15th. By the time the storm cycle was over with, the Lookout Pass ski resort had reported 21 of total snowfall. Visibility was reduced with slick road conditions reported through much of the weekend. Power outages were reported at Lookout Pass during the morning of Nov. 16th, but the reasoning was not reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 170 kt upper level jet dove south out of British Columbia into Washington and Oregon by early Saturday November 16th which provided strong lift and precipitation across north-central Idaho into western Montana. An arctic cold front brought gusty winds and heavy snow at times across Glacier National park and along US-2 from West Glacier to Marias Pass on the 16th.  As the orientation of the upper level jet became more northwesterly by the 17th and moved off to the north and east, showers were felt across much of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway 471 and Thompson Pass were closed for a time on Nov. 16th due to hazardous travel conditions and the Sanders County sheriff office advised people not to travel.  Numerous vehicle slide-offs were reported on I-90 due to snow and ice during the morning of Nov. 16th. The COOP observer located 2 mi. northwest of Heron reported 7.4 of snow with 0.94 of precipitation on Nov. 16th. Also the COOP observer located 1 mi. west of Haugan measured a total of 12 of total snow with 1.20 of precipitation between Nov. 16 and Nov. 18. A NWS employee driving on I-90 east of St. Regis at 6:49 pm MST on Nov. 16th reported less than 100 ft. visibility with heavy snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 170 kt upper level jet dove south out of British Columbia into Washington and Oregon by early Saturday November 16th which provided strong lift and precipitation across north-central Idaho into western Montana. EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bearmouth MTDOT web cam on Interstate 90 at milepost 145.8 indicated snow-covered road conditions for at least 3 hours during the morning hours on Nov. 16.  The MacDonald Pass road sensor located at milepost 27.9 on US-12 reported periods of snow between Nov. 16-17 with strong gusty winds to 35 mph. Web cams at MacDonald Pass indicated periods of blowing snow with limited visibility.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 170 kt upper level jet dove south out of British Columbia into Washington and Oregon by early Saturday November 16th which provided strong lift and precipitation across north-central Idaho into western Montana. An arctic cold front brought gusty winds and heavy snow at times across Glacier National park and along US-2 from West Glacier to Marias Pass on the 16th.  As the orientation of the upper level jet became more northwesterly by the 17th and moved off to the north and east, showers were felt across much of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Pike Creek SNOTEL located near Marias Pass reported a storm total snowfall of 15 while a mesonet sensor located 6 mi. southwest of the pass measured 12 of snow. The Essex web cam reported snow with periods of low visibility while the Montana DOT road reports stated that US-2 between Essex and Marias Pass experienced blowing snow and reduced visibility on the morning of the 16th. On November 16, an arctic cold front moved west through Marias Pass between 12:45 pm and 1:15 pm and through Essex between 2:46 pm and 3:46 pm MST. This front brought gusty winds between 20 and 30 mph for several hours, causing impacts of reduced visibility, snow and blowing snow to travel along the US-2 corridor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad area of moisture and an embedded shortwave trof moved northeastward through Central Idaho bringing widespread strong thunderstorms, as well as scattered severe storms, to Central Montana. The only area that escaped the strong storms was the Northern and Southern Rocky Mountain Fronts as the best severe weather parameters lined up over southwest and central Montana counties. Storm activity gradually decreased from west to east with just a few residual showers overnight.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds of 62KT (71MPH) were measured at Jefferson City, MT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Red Shale Fire began 4:30 p.m. on July 18th. The fire burned much of the heavy downed and standing dead timber and pockets of thick young lodge pole pines regenerated from the 1988 Gates Park Fire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Red Shale Fire was reported by Beartop Lookout at 4:30 p.m. on July 18th. The fire remained within the 1988 Gates Park Fire perimeter. It had worked its way up Frazier Creek and Route Creek where it crossed to the east of the North Fork Sun River in the Bob Marshall Wilderness. The fire reduced much of the heavy downed and standing dead timber and pockets of thick young lodge pole pines regenerated from the 1988 Gates Park Fire. The Red Shale Fire was declared controlled on September 26 2013 at 4 pm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low dropped out of British Columbia on Sunday, October 27th and sank south to Nevada by the morning of the 28th. This allowed an arctic air-mass to spill over the Continental Divide causing strong down-slope winds to occur on the west side of the Mission Mountains. As a result, multiple reports of trees were reported on the east and southeastern side of Flathead Lake. Near blizzard conditions across Silver Bow County caused numerous automobile accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A person from the public in Columbia Falls reported a wind gust to 62 mph at 5:35 pm MDT on Oct. 27th according to their personal weather station. The Flathead Electric Cooperative reported nearly 800 power outages from the storm in the Columbia Falls and Marion areas.  They reported approximately 5,000 co-op members lost power, most through the night, as trees or limbs hit power lines. The ASOS weather sensor at the Glacier Park International Airport measured a wind gust to 48 mph from the northeast at 4:44 pm MDT on Oct 27th.

A vigorous moist Pacific system brought snow to the higher elevations of western Montana impacting mountain passes including Lookout and Lost Trail.  Impacts such as slick roads were reported at pass level by late evening Saturday November 2nd and continued through Monday the 4th. The frontal system brought heavy precipitation (up to 1.60) across the higher elevations of western Montana. Anywhere from 8 to 12 inches of snow were reported in the mountains while valley locations received a trace to 4 inches. A stalled out arctic front brought heavy snow to US-2 from Essex to Marias Pass between Nov. 3 & 4.  A second cold-core upper level trough provided strong unstable conditions that helped to trigger winter waterspouts on Flathead Lake and intense valley-level snow showers on Nov. 4th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A cold core upper level trough dropped out of British Columbia providing the necessary lift and instability to trigger showers across western Montana early on November 4th. The unstable atmosphere saw a 24 degree difference between Flathead Lake water temperatures(8.9 deg. C) and at 9,665 ft. MSL(700 millibars) -15 deg. C.  A convergent boundary set up southwest of Wood's Bay with cool northerly winds coming out of the Flathead Valley and relatively mild southerly winds moving across the lake. This boundary provided the vertical lift to develop the band of graupel and snow showers.  A waterspout was spotted by passers-by traveling on MT-83 near mile-marker 24.  Viewing the photos and video that Mike and Dana Marchetti took, it appears that there could have been multiple vortices based on darkened shadows in the vicinity of the main vortex. The waterspout remained over Flathead Lake and no damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous moist Pacific system brought snow to the higher elevations of western Montana impacting mountain passes including Lookout and Lost Trail.  Impacts such as slick roads were reported at pass level by late evening Saturday November 2nd and continued through Monday the 4th. The frontal system brought heavy precipitation (up to 1.60) across the higher elevations of western Montana. Anywhere from 8 to 12 inches of snow were reported in the mountains while valley locations received a trace to 4 inches. A stalled out arctic front brought heavy snow to US-2 from Essex to Marias Pass between Nov. 3 & 4.  A second cold-core upper level trough provided strong unstable conditions that helped to trigger winter waterspouts on Flathead Lake and intense valley-level snow showers on Nov. 4th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP observer in West Glacier reported a storm total snowfall of 7.3 between Nov. 2nd and Nov. 4th with 0.85 of precipitation. The COOP observer at the Hungry Horse Dam reported 4 of snow with 1.07 of precipitation while a citizen in the town of Hungry Horse measured 6 of snow by 10:30 am MST on Monday, Nov. 4th. A person from the public located 1 mile southeast of Swan Lake estimated that 4 inches had fallen by 10:24 am MST on the 4th. They reported that MT-83 was snow-covered.  This snowfall was most likely connected with the snow band that developed over Flathead Lake just southwest of Wood's Bay.

A vigorous moist Pacific system brought snow to the higher elevations of western Montana impacting mountain passes including Lookout and Lost Trail.  Impacts such as slick roads were reported at pass level by late evening Saturday November 2nd and continued through Monday the 4th. The frontal system brought heavy precipitation (up to 1.60) across the higher elevations of western Montana. Anywhere from 8 to 12 inches of snow were reported in the mountains while valley locations received a trace to 4 inches. A stalled out arctic front brought heavy snow to US-2 from Essex to Marias Pass between Nov. 3 & 4.  A second cold-core upper level trough provided strong unstable conditions that helped to trigger winter waterspouts on Flathead Lake and intense valley-level snow showers on Nov. 4th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy mountain snowfall developed on Saturday Nov. 2nd across northwest Montana. The Hawkins Lake SNOTEL at 6,450 ft. located in extreme northwest Lincoln County reported 1 per hour snowfall rates on the 2nd from 2 to 11 pm MDT with 9 inches of snowfall. This sensor reported 13 total storm snowfall with 1.60 of precipitation through 4 am MST on Nov. 4th. The COOP observer located 9 miles north-northeast of Yaak reported a storm total snowfall of 2.4 with 1.04 of precipitation. On Sunday Nov. 3rd at 7:17 pm MST, a weather spotter reported moderate snow with visibilities less than one half mile from Marion to North Star Meadows and slick conditions on US-2.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Montana DOT at Lookout Pass on I-90 reported a storm total snowfall of 8 inches from Saturday evening Nov. 2nd through Monday morning the 4th. No accidents were reported, but road surfaces were reported to be slick especially by late evening on the 2nd when road temperatures cooled to freezing and below. The nearby Lookout SNOTEL reported 1.00 of total precipitation during the event. The COOP station 1 mile west of Haugan reported a storm total snowfall of  2 with 0.75 of precipitation.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow showers developed on the back side of an exiting upper level trough across northwest Montana by Sunday afternoon November 3rd. With warm temperatures initially the showers were not much of an impact, but as they grew in size, visibilities dropped down to less than one half mile or less at times, air & road temperatures fell to near freezing and roads became snow-covered and slick. A weather spotter in Columbia Falls reported 4 of total snow with a majority of falling as moderate to heavy snow from 3:37 to 7:37 pm MST on the 3rd. Other snowfall amounts include: 2 5 miles north-northwest of Creston, 3 at the Glacier Park Int. Airport and 2.2 at Bigfork/Ferndale.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple of disturbances moved through the area bringing isolated to scattered|showers for the lower elevations with numerous snow showers for the mountains. The main trough moved into western Montana Saturday, reaching Central Montana by early evening and eastern Montana after midnight. The associated strong Canadian cold front moved south of the border during the morning, reaching near a Helena to Harlowton line by late afternoon.  Strong winds were expected with the Canadian cold front over the Rockies and surface pressure falls in Alberta helped to strengthen down-slope flow along the Rocky Mountain Front. High winds developed east of the Rockies in a few locations as a result. A winter storm warning was put into effect above 5000 feet for the Rocky Mountain Front and some locations a little further south. A winter weather advisory was issued for the Little Belt Mountains and selected mountains and passes over southwest Montana into Sunday. A winter weather advisory for lower elevations of the Northern Rocky Mountain Front was issued into Sunday as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds measuring 51KT (59MPH) were reported 1 mi E of East Glacier Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few thunderstorms containing large hail and very heavy rain occurred across portions of the Billings Forecast Area. Hail the size of baseballs along with some road washouts due to heavy rain were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was significant crop damage due to the hail and strong winds. The largest hailstones were estimated to be 3 inches in diameter, which is slightly larger than baseball size. Hail drifts were still 4 inches deep at 6 pm. Power outages also occurred in the area.

An upper level low dropped out of British Columbia on Sunday, October 27th and sank south to Nevada by the morning of the 28th. This allowed an arctic air-mass to spill over the Continental Divide causing strong down-slope winds to occur on the west side of the Mission Mountains. As a result, multiple reports of trees were reported on the east and southeastern side of Flathead Lake. Near blizzard conditions across Silver Bow County caused numerous automobile accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A person located at Montana Tech in Butte reported heavy snow that brought limited visibilities and impeded traffic.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A local newspaper reporter tweeted at 7:57 am MST on November 4th that slick roads had caused several accidents from Frenchtown to just east of US-93 in Missoula, or between mile markers 92 and 98 on Interstate 90. The MT DOT Ninemile sensor reported snow falling continuously from 6 pm MST on Sunday evening Nov. 3rd through 11:45 am MST on Monday Nov. 4th with road temperatures ranging between 28 and 32 degrees F.  Nearby snowfall amounts include: 1.5 Evaro Hill, 1.3 2 mi. west of Bonner, 3 Arlee, 0.3 Missoula Int. Airport, and 1.5 Seeley Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The seasons first arctic outbreak and combined winter storm moved into the Billings Forecast Area during the first week of December. Mild temperatures ended the month of November and continued through the morning of the 2nd. A strong disturbance moved across the area during the afternoon of the 2nd. This disturbance combined with temperatures around 50 degrees for most of the day, resulted in a period of light to moderate rain for a few hours. An arctic front moved into the area by late in the afternoon turning the rain to snow. Temperatures quickly dropped into the teens and single digits during the overnight hours. The initial wet snow that fell onto already wet pavement from the rain quickly froze which resulted in very icy road conditions. The snow, which was heavy at times, continued through the 3rd before ending on the 4th of December.  However, bitterly cold temperatures continued through the 8th with daytime highs only in the single digits above and below zero with overnight lows in the teens below to 30s below zero.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 5 to 7 inches along with blowing snow and icy roads was reported across the county.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Montana DOT reported heavy snow Sunday afternoon Nov. 3rd through the morning hours of Monday Nov. 4th on US-2. The snowfall was especially heavy between Essex (mile-marker 180) and Stanton Creek (mile-marker 192) along US-2.  They estimated that up to 2 feet of snow fell during the storm in these areas. The Marias Pass HADS weather sensor at 5233 ft. reported 0.50 of precipitation with temperatures falling into the lower 20's. Using a 20:1 average snow to liquid ratio Marias Pass could have seen a foot of snow.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public estimated wind gusts up to 70 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong daytime heating and an unstable atmosphere with a favorable wind profile allowed strong to severe thunderstorms to develop across far eastern Montana before moving into North Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported quarter-sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As surface dewpoints from the southwest reached 70 degrees and daytime temperatures soared above 90 degrees, a lee-side surface trough of low pressure focused the development of thunderstorms with very strong updrafts.  One particular super-cell thunderstorm displayed textbook features and characteristics which spawned a Tornado which moved eastward across northern Roosevelt county.  Tornado damage survey conducted the following morning resulted in an EF-2 rating.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cooperative observer reported quarter-sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 3-year-old girl who was injured in a highway collision on December 11th died of her injuries on December 30th. The collision occurred just north of the train crossing underpass on Highway 212/310 in icy conditions. The girl's mother lost control of her car on an S-curve crossing the center line. A 19-year-old man driving a pickup truck hit the passenger side of the vehicle. The man was uninjured but the woman was hospitalized for injuries.
A mid-level southwest 50 knot jet maximum combined with high moisture and very warm temperatures provided the prime environment for strong supercells. These cells brought both severe wind and hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On July 17, 2013, a multicellular thunderstorm developed a gustnado causing a roof to be torn off of a closed garage and thrown into a power pole. The power pole broke off leaving live wires on the ground. Another shed immediately downstream was torn apart and thrown across the yard. The path of the damage was about 200 yards long and 50 to 100 feet wide. A neighbor watching the event occur from a few feet away, reported swirling debris for at least 10 to 15 seconds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The seasons first arctic outbreak and combined winter storm moved into the Billings Forecast Area during the first week of December. Mild temperatures ended the month of November and continued through the morning of the 2nd. A strong disturbance moved across the area during the afternoon of the 2nd. This disturbance combined with temperatures around 50 degrees for most of the day, resulted in a period of light to moderate rain for a few hours. An arctic front moved into the area by late in the afternoon turning the rain to snow. Temperatures quickly dropped into the teens and single digits during the overnight hours. The initial wet snow that fell onto already wet pavement from the rain quickly froze which resulted in very icy road conditions. The snow, which was heavy at times, continued through the 3rd before ending on the 4th of December.  However, bitterly cold temperatures continued through the 8th with daytime highs only in the single digits above and below zero with overnight lows in the teens below to 30s below zero.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 6 to 12 inches along with blowing snow and icy roads was reported.

A potent mid-level cut-off low moved from southwest Idaho to southern Lemhi county, Idaho by 6 am MDT Thursday October 3rd and then tracked southeast into southeast Idaho by Thursday night. The storm intensified even more as it ingested Gulf of Mexico moisture over Wyoming, allowing for wrap-around moisture to make it as far north as Butte, MT Thursday night. The storm brought snow, blowing snow and slick road conditions to portions of southwest Montana in the higher elevations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A person from the public located 19 miles south-southeast of Butte at 6,600 ft., reported 17 inches of snow (estimated). A weather spotter located one mile to the north reported that power was out for several hours on Thursday morning October 3rd. They also confirmed the report of 17 inches of snow from this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong and moist upper level system interacted with a cold Canadian air mass to bring areas of heavy snow impacting travel across western Montana. The combination of heavy snow and colder temperatures allowed for snow to stick to roadways and cause power outages in some areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lookout Pass webcams revealed heavy snow that stuck to the roadway and reduced visibilities to less than one quarter mile from at least 1400 to 1900 MDT. A vehicle spun-out at the pass blocking I-90 east-bound that brought traffic to a standstill.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Temperatures rose well into the 90s across much of western Montana which allowed a strong dust devil to form north of Rexford. A school was damaged as a result.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public in West Kootenai observed a strong and long-lived dust devil that caused roof damage to the Amish school and cut power momentarily at a warehouse. They said this was the strongest one they had ever experienced there in decades.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough moved across the Pacific Northwest bringing southwest flow and moisture to the region. Winds aloft increased as a strong pressure gradient set up along the Rocky Mountain Front. An associated cold front moved across the Continental Divide helping enhance wind speeds and dynamics over the Rocky Mountain Front. As a result, snow developed and become heavy at times above 5000 feet. The system later moved into Manitoba leaving the area under unstable cyclonic flow which allowed scattered mountain snow showers to continue.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow measuring 6.0 inches in 12 hours was reported by the Madison Plateau SNOTEL. Elevation 7750 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A high pressure ridge over the region was pushed east by a steadily advancing upper-level trough extending from Central Alberta to northern California. Small pockets of instability and moisture associated with a transient shortwave combined with generally unstable conditions to produce scattered thunderstorms along the Continental Divide and Southwest MT before spreading into Central MT during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail measuring 1.00 inches (quarter sized) was reported 26 mi NW of Santa Rita, MT. Mix of quarter and pea sized hail accumulated to approximately a foot deep around buildings during storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Red Shale Fire began at 4:30 p.m. on July 18th. The fire burned much of the heavy downed and standing dead timber and pockets of thick young lodge pole pines regenerated from the 1988 Gates Park Fire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Red Shale Fire was reported by Beartop Lookout at 4:30 p.m. on July 18th. The fire remains within the 1988 Gates Park Fire perimeter. It had worked its way up Frazier Creek and Route Creek where it crossed to the east of the North Fork Sun River in the Bob Marshall Wilderness. The fire reduced much of the heavy downed and standing dead timber and pockets of thick young lodge pole pines regenerated from the 1988 Gates Park Fire.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow measuring 7.4 inches in 10 hours was reported 2 mi SE of Montana City, MT. Elevation 4800 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Instability and warm temperatures, associated with a shortwave trough moving through Central Montana from southwest to northeast, brought scattered showers and thunderstorms to portions of North Central Montana. Parameters were generally marginal for severe storms but a couple cells did have strong wind gusts, brief heavy rain, and isolated large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail measuring 1.50 inches (ping pong ball sized) was reported along highway 191 near mile marker 29 approximately 17 mi SSE of Grass Range, MT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Convective activity developed early in the afternoon with thunderstorms rapidly developing over West and Southwest Montana in response to an upper trough moving across Northwest Montana into southern Alberta. Though jet streak energy supported the system, convective parameters were not overly impressive. Additionally, precipitable water values were very high across the CWA translating to heavy rain with some isolated to scattered severe storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding reported in Bannack State Park created a small debris flow and 5 injuries were reported by Beaverhead County DES Manager. Heavy rain of .77 inches was reported in about 30 minutes in the park.

Strong daytime heating along with 40-50 knots of southwesterly mid-level flow resulted in a very unstable atmosphere that was conducive for supercell thunderstorms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two Pacific low pressure systems brought abundant moisture to the Billings Forecast Area during the end of the month. This resulted in flooding of small streams and creeks, as well as across burn scar areas. The two storm systems resulted in above normal precipitation for the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observations across the area indicated widespread 3 to 6 inches of rain fell across the area over the course of a few days. Emergency management reported that Home Creek along Highway 212 experienced some flooding. In addition, flooding of small streams and creeks occurred across the area along with debris flows on the Ash Creek burn scar area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist surface layer combined with a near-stationary, large area of low pressure over the western states to produce slow-moving, efficient downpours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall of up to 4 inches left up to a foot of water 100 yards across on Highway 253 near mile marker 42, causing the road to be closed down for several hours. The road was closed by the Sheriff's Department for several hours. An area farmer reported his fields were a large lake with water running across them.

Strong daytime heating along with 40-50 knots of southwesterly mid-level flow resulted in a very unstable atmosphere that was conducive for supercell thunderstorms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As surface dewpoints from the southwest reached 70 degrees and daytime temperatures soared above 90 degrees, a lee-side surface trough of low pressure focused the development of thunderstorms with very strong updrafts.  One particular super-cell thunderstorm displayed textbook features and characteristics which spawned a Tornado which moved eastward across northern Roosevelt county.  Tornado damage survey conducted the following morning resulted in an EF-2 rating.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado was first spotted about 19 miles north of Wolf Point and the NWS Storm Damage Team found downed tree limbs and a de-barked tree trunk.  As it moved eastward, the tornado rolled heavy hay bails, snapped a road-sign post and flipped a loose yard shed.  A few miles west of the Poplar River and just north of County Road 2042, the Beck Farm sustained the most damage involving roofs peeled away and blown off of several farm and storage buildings.  Winds here were estimated at 115 to 120 mph.  After crossing over the Poplar River, the tornado downed a large cottonwood tree, lifted and moved irrigation pipes, and snapped 9 power poles, then dissipated near the intersection of County Roads 2041 and 1041, about 17 miles north of Poplar.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A well built, newer Morton Building had part of the roof peeled off by rear-flank downdraft winds from a tornadic supercell. The EF-2 tornado was two miles south of this location.

Small pockets of instability and moisture associated with a transient shortwave combined with generally unstable conditions to produce scattered thunderstorms along the Continental Divide and Southwest MT before spreading into Central MT during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds measured at 51KT (59MPH) were reported 1 mi SSW of Rudyard, MT.

A local business northwest of Eureka reported at least 30 trees toppled due to the strong wind. Also the combination of wind and hail damaged the greens at a nearby golf course.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid-level southwest 50 knot jet maximum combined with high moisture and very warm temperatures provided the prime environment for strong supercells. These cells brought both severe wind and hail.

A person from the public estimated penny sized hail just northwest of Woodworth that lasted for 5 minutes. The Missoula Doppler radar observed a three body scatter spike between 12 and 18 thousand feet MSL with this storm which is indicative of large hail.

The hail covered the ground to a depth of 2 inches in spots. At least 10 windows were broken. Paint was chipped off from homes. Other damage included a greenhouse roof, vehicle windows and vegetation.

A couple storms, mainly in and around the Helena Valley, became severe with strong wind gusts and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail measuring 1.75 inches was reported 9 mi NNE of Helena, MT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low-pressure system and a stalled surface front amid a moderately-unstable atmosphere became the focus for perisistent thunderstorms during the late-night hours, some of which became severe over central portions of northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported penny-sized hail and 60 mph winds among nearly 2 inches of rainfall in just 20 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough tracked into northwest Montana by late afternoon on July 8th. The leading 50 knot mid-level southwest jet maximum moved towards the Bitterroot Valley by late morning helping to initiate a few hail producing supercell thunderstorms. A second round of thunderstorms developed by the afternoon across northwest Montana producing more hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A supercell enhanced by the Bitterroot Mountains tracked east-northeast at approximately 43 mph producing 1 inch hail in Victor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough tracked into northwest Montana by late afternoon on July 8th. The leading 50 knot mid-level southwest jet maximum moved towards the Bitterroot Valley by late morning helping to initiate a few hail producing supercell thunderstorms. A second round of thunderstorms developed by the afternoon across northwest Montana producing more hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A right-moving supercell thunderstorm tracked across the Mission Mountains moving east around 43 mph.  A member from the public 9 miles south of Condon reported golf ball size hail that tore branches and leaves off trees and covered the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper-level disturbance from the pacific northwest combined with an unstable low-level atmosphere to create many severe thunderstorms which progressed eastward across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported hail up to the size of hen eggs fell for 10 minutes, breaking a skylight and several windows.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper-level disturbance from the pacific northwest combined with an unstable low-level atmosphere to create many severe thunderstorms which progressed eastward across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported hail up to the size of golf balls broke windows, damaged siding, damaged a camper, and destroyed a pea crop.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper-level disturbance from the pacific northwest combined with an unstable low-level atmosphere to create many severe thunderstorms which progressed eastward across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported that hail up to the size of baseballs fell for over 10 minutes, breaking windows and stripping most of the leaves from a tree.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper-level disturbance from the pacific northwest combined with an unstable low-level atmosphere to create many severe thunderstorms which progressed eastward across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported quarter-sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A descending cold front, moderate instability, and strong wind sherar set the stage for the development of many severe thunderstorms moving southeast across most of northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported quarter-sized hail covering the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper-level disturbance and a very moist surface layer combined to produce numerous severe thunderstorms from the lee side of the northern Rockies through the northern high plains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Comertown Turn-Off MT-5 DOT site recorded an 86 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist and unstable air mass combined with an upper-level disturbance to create a few fast-moving and efficient severe thunderstorms across Phillips County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported quarter-sized hail with heavy rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Conditions were favorable for large hail and damaging winds across portions of the Billings Forecast Area. Surface heating, low level moisture and a strong upper level disturbance all contributed to the development of severe thunderstorms across Southeast Montana and Northern Wyoming.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding occurred along Buckskin Road near Harper Coulee in the Dahl Fire burn scar area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few thunderstorms moved across Yellowstone and Musselshell Counties during the evening of June 2nd. Some hail was reported but the main threat from these thunderstorms was flash flooding, mainly over burn scar areas.

A few thunderstorms moved across Yellowstone and Musselshell Counties during the evening of June 2nd. Some hail was reported but the main threat from these thunderstorms was flash flooding, mainly over burn scar areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were numerous spotter reports and DES (Department of Emergency Services) reports of flash flooding along Halfbreed Creek south of Roundup near the Wilson Burn area. The DES also reported high water and debris over numerous roads in the Dahl and Wilson Burn areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few thunderstorms moved across Yellowstone and Musselshell Counties during the evening of June 2nd. Some hail was reported but the main threat from these thunderstorms was flash flooding, mainly over burn scar areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant flooding occurred on Dean Creek along the Golden Valley/Musselshell County line. Driveways and fences were destroyed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough centered offshore of Washington state pushed eastward and allowed the Northern Rockies to be in the southerly flow regime favorable for thunderstorms.  A 50 knot mid-level south to south-southwest jet maximum (500 mb) moved into the region by late afternoon and helped to trigger storms over the higher terrain of western Montana. Several storms had rather impressive radar reflectivities while only a few were able produce observable strong gusty winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong thunderstorm developed just north of Essex and as it moved towards Glacier National Park it released strong gusty winds up to 55 mph. These winds toppled several trees up to 24 inches in diameter in the Apgar Village and Fish Creek campground. A few of the trees fell onto power lines while one large cottonwood branch fell on top of a  Park Service storage building in Apgar Village, causing minor damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few thunderstorms moved across Yellowstone and Musselshell Counties during the evening of June 2nd. Some hail was reported but the main threat from these thunderstorms was flash flooding, mainly over burn scar areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency Managers and trained spotters reported flooding and significant debris flows over and near the Dahl, Delphia and Wilson Burn Scar areas. The city of Roundup experienced street flooding. Many secondary roads in the area were washed out. Flooding also occurred on Dean Creek just southwest of Roundup resulting in damage to driveways and fences.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A corridor of warm, moist air at the surface combined with an upper level disturbance and a stationary frontal boundary to produce severe thunderstorms across northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported nickel-sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A corridor of warm, moist air at the surface combined with an upper level disturbance and a stationary frontal boundary to produce severe thunderstorms across northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported wind gusts of 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A corridor of warm, moist air at the surface combined with an upper level disturbance and a stationary frontal boundary to produce severe thunderstorms across northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported wind gusts of 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated thunderstorm produced a few weak and brief tornadoes near the Montana/North Dakota border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported a tornado that touched down twice along Big Hill Road in extreme northeast Fallon County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An overall wet spring season and recent heavy rainfall combined with additional storms during early June to rise the level of the Milk River from Dodson eventually through Glasgow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Milk River at Tampico (TMPM8) rose above its flood stage of 23 feet at 3:40 AM MST on June 6th, crested at 24.69 feet at 5:00 AM MST on June 11th, then fell back down below flood stage at 12:15 PM MST on June 13th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An overall wet spring season and recent heavy rainfall combined with additional storms during early June to rise the level of the Milk River from Dodson eventually through Glasgow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Milk River at Dodson (DMRM8) rose above its flood stage of 23 feet at 2:30 PM MST on June 4th, crested at 25.95 feet at 4:40 PM MST on June 5th, then fell back down below flood stage at 10:45 PM MST on June 7th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Significant rainfall over several days caused many small streams and creeks to come out of their banks across much of the Musselshell River drainage basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Musselshell River at Mosby (MSBM8) rose above its flood stage of 10 feet at 11:10 PM MST on June 3rd, crested at 11.72 feet at 7:50 AM MST on June 3rd, then feel back down below flood stage at 1:10 AM MST on June 6th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Steering winds at both the surface and upper levels and available moisture combined to create efficient thunderstorms, a few of which became severe after emerging east of the northern Rockies.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cow Creek mesonet site recorded a 65 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Steering winds at both the surface and upper levels and available moisture combined to create efficient thunderstorms, a few of which became severe after emerging east of the northern Rockies.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Charlie Creek mesonet site recorded a 62 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Steering winds at both the surface and upper levels and available moisture combined to create efficient thunderstorms, a few of which became severe after emerging east of the northern Rockies.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A disturbance moved across the Billings Forecast Area during the late morning hours on the 13th that resulted in severe thunderstorms that lasted into the early morning hours of the 14th.  Strong daytime heating along with 40-50 knots of southwesterly mid-level flow resulted in a very unstable atmosphere that was conducive for supercell thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several roads in the Dahl and Wilson Burn Scar areas were impacted by flash flooding, including Harper Coulee, Buckskin, Shortcut, Hampton, McCord and Aspen. Many private roads experienced significant erosion due to very heavy rainfall. Radar estimated 1 to 2 inches in an hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous severe thunderstorms developed during the late afternoon and early evening hours on the 19th.  The severe thunderstorms occurred to the north and west of Billings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding occurred across portions of the Wilson and Dahl Fire burn scar areas. Radar estimated between 1 and 2 inches of rain fell in an hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous severe thunderstorms developed during the late afternoon and early evening hours on the 19th.  The severe thunderstorms occurred to the north and west of Billings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fast moving water, approximately 1 foot deep, flooded private roadways.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough centered offshore of Washington state pushed eastward and allowed the Northern Rockies to be in the southerly flow regime favorable for thunderstorms.  A 50 knot mid-level south to south-southwest jet maximum (500 mb) moved into the region by late afternoon and helped to trigger storms over the higher terrain of western Montana. Several storms had rather impressive radar reflectivities while only a few were able produce observable strong gusty winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong thunderstorm cell developed 15 miles south of Seeley Lake and by 446 pm MDT it reached its climax in strength reaching up to 44 kft MSL in height. The precipitation core of the thunderstorm dropped after that producing a gust front that reached the Seeley Lake area a little after 5 pm. Multiple Lodgepole pine trees, 50 to 80 feet tall and 8 to 10 inches in diameter, were toppled by strong gusty winds.  The Lodgepole were widely spaced, and with very little root structure on that variety of tree, were particularly susceptible to strong winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A single line of thunderstorms moved from Park County to Yellowstone County during the early evening hours of the 29th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mudslide was reported just north of Big Timber on Highway 191. There were additional reports of flash flooding from Big Timber to Melville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An overall wet spring season and recent heavy rainfall combined with additional storms during early June to rise the level of the Milk River from Dodson eventually through Glasgow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Milk River at the Glasgow Highway 24 bridge (GLWM8) rose above its flood stage of 25 feet at 7:40 PM MST on June 4th, crested at 27.82 feet at 5:00 PM MST on June 11th, then fell back down below flood stage at 8:00 AM MST on June 18th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few thunderstorms producing very strong winds moved across the foothills locations of the Beartooth/Absaroka Mountains.

An overall wet spring season and recent heavy rainfall primarilly over Phillips county combined with additional storms during early June to increase the levels along creeks and streams feeding into the Milk River, including Peoples Creek near the town of Dodson.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peoples Creek, west of the town of Dodson, rose out of its banks on June 4th and covered the surrounding low-lying areas, including Montana Highway 8 and US Highway 2. Law enforcement officials set off a quarter-mile section of US Highway 2, directing vehicles to slowly tread a depth of 3 inches of water until the water receeded on June 6th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An overall wet spring season and recent heavy rainfall combined with additional storms during early June to increase the levels along creeks and streams feeding into the Milk River, including Beaver Creek near the town of Hinsdale.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Beaver Creek near the town of Hinsdale rose out of its banks on June 4th and flooded the surrounding lower-lying areas, county roads, and fields, south of US Highway 2 between the towns of Saco and Hinsdale. Even as the level of Beaver Creek near Hinsdale was later receeding on June 6th, flooding was still occurring south of Saco.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper-level disturbance and a very moist surface layer combined to produce numerous severe thunderstorms from the lee side of the northern Rockies through the northern high plains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS employee located 2.2 miles NNW of Glasgow reported wind gusts up to 70 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent Pacific storm system emerging from the northern Rockies combined with an arctic air mass from Canada to develop into a winter storm over northeast montana with Blizzard conditions developing for some locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several snowfall reports across the area including the NWS office in Glasgow ranged from 6 to 8 inches with 8.5 inches being reported at Hinsdale.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved across the Billings Forecast Area during the afternoon of the 3rd. Sunny skies prevailed for several hours before the frontal passage allowing temperatures to warm into the 50s and 60s resulting in the atmosphere becoming very unstable. A few thunderstorms developed along the front and merged into a small MCS.  The thunderstorms contained frequent lightning along with heavy rain that quickly turned to heavy snow, graupel and small hail for about a 45 minute period as temperatures quickly dropped into the 30s. In addition, sustained winds of 40 mph along with gusts of 60 mph to around 70 mph resulted in some property damage. Billings reported numerous power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Columbus woman died on the night of the 27th when the sport-utility vehicle she was driving crashed into a semitrailer on Interstate 90 between Billings and Columbus due to icy conditions. The woman was driving westbound about eight miles east of Columbus on I-90 when, shortly after 11pm, she lost control and spun out while going through a slight curve in the road. The jeep struck a guardrail on the north side of the interstate and went back into the roadway. At the same time, a semi came around the curve and swerved into the passing lane to avoid the Jeep. The jeep crashed into the passenger side of the semi. The woman was pronounced dead at the scene when emergency crews arrived.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extended period of extreme cold developed over western Montana in early December. It was the coldest air mass in more than 4 years to affect the region. A secondary push of arctic air, much colder than the first, moved into the region late Thursday, Dec. 5 and Dec. 6, which helped to bring gusty winds, and very low wind chills to canyon and mountain gap locations. Flathead Lake and nearby shores saw lake effect snow activity develop on Dec. 4 and continue through Dec. 8, bringing anywhere from a trace up to 10 inches of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Fielding RAWS, located 4.4 miles southwest of Marias Pass, reported air temperatures between -20 and -28 F during the mornings and evenings from 3:42 am MST on Dec. 6, through 2:42 am MST on Dec. 8. The HADS sensor at Marias Pass also measured very cold air temperatures: -27.5 F at 3 am MST Dec. 5, -30.9 F at 7:30 am MST Dec. 6, and -41.2 F at 7:45 am MST Dec. 7. A person from the public located 8 miles southwest of Marias Pass, reported frozen pipes on Dec. 6.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extended period of extreme cold developed over western Montana in early December. It was the coldest air mass in more than 4 years to affect the region. A secondary push of arctic air, much colder than the first, moved into the region late Thursday, Dec. 5 and Dec. 6, which helped to bring gusty winds, and very low wind chills to canyon and mountain gap locations. Flathead Lake and nearby shores saw lake effect snow activity develop on Dec. 4 and continue through Dec. 8, bringing anywhere from a trace up to 10 inches of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: With arctic air entrenched over western Montana, convergent winds developed over a relatively warm Flathead Lake, allowing for lake effect snow (LES) to develop. Lake temperatures ranged from 32 to 36 degrees Fahrenheit during this event. Depending on where the convergent boundary set up, locations would receive anywhere from a dusting up to 10 inches of snow. During the morning of Dec. 5, a very intense west to east oriented LES band, 1 to 2 miles wide, formed north of Yellow Bay. An estimated 8 to 10 inches fell 1 mile south of Wood's Bay along Hwy-35, especially between mile-marker 18 and 22. An interesting note during this heavy snowfall is that the consistency of the snowflakes were observed to be large dendrites. As a secondary push of colder air moved in later on Dec. 5, the LES band shifted further south and light snow was reported between MM-3 and 5 on Hwy-35 just south of Finley Point. As indicated correctly by the high resolution WRF-ARW model forecast, the LES band became more north to south oriented. It brought up to 3 inches of snow from Polson to Big Arm between MM 57-69 on US-93 through 8:37 am MST on Dec. 6. The LES band continued to affect the Polson area through Dec. 7, bringing less than one inch of snow, which made roads slick. The Polson sheriff reported 2 accidents due to slick conditions at 12:51 pm on Dec. 7. They indicated that the snow was coming down as very fine grains and air temperatures ranged from 0 to -10 F. By the evening of Dec. 7, the LES band had shifted further to the east affecting the east and southeast side of Flathead Lake. A weather spotter located 8 miles north of Finley Point, measured 1 inch of snow between 6 and 6:30 pm MST on Dec. 7. An estimated 8 inches of heavy snow was reported near mile-markers 80-100 on US-93 near Rollins at 5 pm MST on Dec. 8. An estimated 8 inches of snow was reported by MT DOT, 2 miles north of Bigfork at 6:30 pm MST on Dec. 8.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad weather disturbance that moved across Montana and Idaho brought snow and wind to portions of the Billings Forecast Area. Tight Pressure gradients through the Judith Gap area resulted in strong winds along with reduced visibilities due to blowing snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds of at least 40 mph were reported during the late morning through the early afternoon hours. Reduced visibilities due to blowing snow were also an issue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frigid arctic air mass combined with a tight surface pressure gradient to allow very cold winds to spread across northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports of wind chills at or below negative 40 were common with the longest stretch of frigid wind chills being reported at the Comertown DOT site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frigid arctic air mass combined with a tight surface pressure gradient to allow very cold winds to spread across northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Automated observations in this area were unavailable, but the AWOS site at Val Marie, just across the Canadian border reported wind chills at or below negative 40 for about 4 hours.  And, the stations just east in Valley County reported wind chills of 40 below zero for more than 3 hours.

A secondary push of arctic air, much colder than the first, moved into the region late Thursday, Dec. 5 and Dec. 6, which helped to bring gusty winds, and very low wind chills to canyon and mountain gap locations. Flathead Lake and nearby shores saw lake effect snow activity develop on Dec. 4 and continue through Dec. 8, bringing anywhere from a trace up to 10 inches of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Georgetown Lake MT DOT sensor reported wind chill values between -20 and -31 F. The coldest air temperature reported was -22 F at 12:25 am MST on Dec. 8. The MT DOT sensor located 7 miles north-northwest of Avon on MT-141, reported wind chill values between -20 to -45 F from Dec. 3-9. The -45 F wind chill occurred at 1:59 am MST on Dec. 7, with a wind speed of 11 mph and air temperature was -22.7 F. The Bert Mooney Airport ASOS recorded its coldest wind chill of -35 F at 1:53 am MST on Dec. 7.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frigid arctic air mass combined with a tight surface pressure gradient to allow very cold winds to spread across northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: ASOS observations at the Glasgow airport recorded wind chills at and below 40 below zero.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extended period of extreme cold developed over western Montana in early December. It was the coldest air mass in more than 4 years to affect the region. A secondary push of arctic air, much colder than the first, moved into the region late Thursday, Dec. 5 and Dec. 6, which helped to bring gusty winds, and very low wind chills to canyon and mountain gap locations. Flathead Lake and nearby shores saw lake effect snow activity develop on Dec. 4 and continue through Dec. 8, bringing anywhere from a trace up to 10 inches of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Essex MT DOT sensor reported wind chill values between -20 and -33 F, with an average wind speed of 9 mph through 11:22 am MST Dec.5.  The peak wind during that time was 17 mph.  It also measured -20 to -32 F wind chill values from 9:16 pm MST Thursday Dec. 5 through 11:31 am MST Friday Dec. 6, with an average wind speed of 7 mph.  The peak wind was 18 mph.  It also reported -20 to -49 F wind chill values from 3:16 pm MST Dec. 6 through 12:20 pm MST Dec. 7, with an average wind speed of 10 mph.  The -49 F wind chill occurred at 7:58 am MST on Dec. 7 with a wind speed of 19 mph. It reported air temperatures between -20 and -22 F from 8:13 pm MST Dec. 7 through 2:16 am MST Dec. 8. The prevailing wind direction throughout this event was from the southeast.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mullan Pass sensor reported -20 to -34 wind chill values with an average wind speed of 8 mph. The -34 F value was observed at 6:53 MST on Dec. 7 when the air temperature was -13 F and the wind speed was 12.7 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extended period of extreme cold developed over western Montana in early December. It was the coldest air mass in more than 4 years to affect the region. A secondary push of arctic air, much colder than the first, moved into the region late Thursday, Dec. 5 and Dec. 6, which helped to bring gusty winds, and very low wind chills to canyon and mountain gap locations. Flathead Lake and nearby shores saw lake effect snow activity develop on Dec. 4 and continue through Dec. 8, bringing anywhere from a trace up to 10 inches of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Stillwater RAWS at 3,116 ft., located 1.1 mile southeast of Olney, reported air temperatures between -20 and -31 F mainly during the late evening and morning hours during this time period. The coldest value of -31 F occurred at 8 am MST on Dec. 7, which happens to be the same temperature recorded by the Olney COOP, and was a record minimum for the day. The Boorman RAWS at 3,963 ft., located 3 miles northwest of Marion reported air temperatures between -20 and -25 F between Dec. 6-8.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extended period of extreme cold developed over western Montana in early December. It was the coldest air mass in more than 4 years to affect the region. A secondary push of arctic air, much colder than the first, moved into the region late Thursday, Dec. 5 and Dec. 6, which helped to bring gusty winds, and very low wind chills to canyon and mountain gap locations. Flathead Lake and nearby shores saw lake effect snow activity develop on Dec. 4 and continue through Dec. 8, bringing anywhere from a trace up to 10 inches of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Missoula International Airport reported wind chill values between -20 and -28 F with an average wind speed of 16 mph. During the morning of Dec. 7, the wind chill values could have ranged between -30 and -35 F across eastern Missoula, especially by Hellgate Canyon where wind speeds were a little stronger than by the airport. Thousands of people attended a championship football game at the University of Montana football stadium, while wind chill values possibly fluctuated between -15 and -20 F at times during the early afternoon on Dec. 7.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For the second time during the 2013/2014 winter season, a strong arctic cold front plowed south and west across western Montana. The initial push of arctic air undercut moist Pacific air causing heavy snow, blowing snow and poor visibility. This arctic outbreak was very similar to those that occurred in early December of 1972 and also in 1956.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Ronan Airport AWOS sensor reported northerly winds to 31 mph with the arctic front passage. Heavy snow, blowing snow reducing visibility, and slick conditions on US-93 from Ronan to Polson were reported by MT DOT at 6:50 pm MST. At 8:22 pm MST, MT DOT reported heavy snow with snow-covered roads from MT-200 west of Dixon to Ravalli and on Highway-212, 4 miles north of Dixon to Moise. Total snowfall ranged from 2 to 4 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For the second time during the 2013/2014 winter season, a strong arctic cold front plowed south and west across western Montana. The initial push of arctic air undercut moist Pacific air causing heavy snow, blowing snow and poor visibility. This arctic outbreak was very similar to those that occurred in early December of 1972 and also in 1956.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The arctic front passed through Missoula a little after 8 pm MST and brought 0.5 of snow, blowing snow with a few northerly wind gusts over 30 mph through 10 pm MST on Dec. 2. Visibilities fell down close to zero briefly with the combination of falling snow and blowing snow.  A person from the public reported strong winds, blowing snow and whiteout conditions on US-93 between Missoula and Lolo around 11:36 pm MST on Dec. 2. Half an inch of snow fell at the Missoula Airport, 1  to 2 fell in Lolo and 3 fell in the Grant Creek area 4 miles north-northwest of Missoula.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tight pressure gradients across Wheatland County resulted in very strong wind gusts across the Judith Gap area.
A potent low pressure system funneled strong winds through central portions of Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Malta South US-191 DOT site measured sustained winds of 40 mph for about 2 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For the second time during the 2013/2014 winter season, a strong arctic cold front plowed south and west across western Montana. The initial push of arctic air undercut moist Pacific air causing heavy snow, blowing snow and poor visibility. This arctic outbreak was very similar to those that occurred in early December of 1972 and also in 1956.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A person from the public in Olney reported heavy snow with blowing snow creating whiteout conditions on US-93 near mile-marker 145. Although snow totals ranged between 2 to 3 inches across the area, this all fell within 2 to 3 hours ahead and with the arctic cold front passage. It was this sudden change with falling temperatures from above freezing to the teens, that caused roads to become very slick with several automobile slide-offs reported by the Flathead County dispatch between Olney and Whitefish. The Eureka Airport AWOS reported visibilities less than or equal to one-quarter mile from 11:56 am to 1:46 pm MST while the City of Eureka webcam indicated falling snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For the second time during the 2013/2014 winter season, a strong arctic cold front plowed south and west across western Montana. The initial push of arctic air undercut moist Pacific air causing heavy snow, blowing snow and poor visibility. This arctic outbreak was very similar to those that occurred in early December of 1972 and also in 1956.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front pushed through MacDonald Pass in the morning and brought periods of heavy snow and blowing snow reducing visibilities to near whiteout at times. The Deer Lodge AWOS reported visibilities less than one-quarter mile for 1.4 hours, while northerly winds gusting to 37 mph occurred as the arctic cold front passed through. A weather spotter at 6,200 ft., 2 miles southwest of Newcomb,  measured 5.5 inches of heavy snow, 5 hours ending at 3:50 pm MST. They also reported that 6 to 10 inches of snow fell across the higher elevations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved across the Billings Forecast Area during the afternoon of the 3rd. Sunny skies prevailed for several hours before the frontal passage allowing temperatures to warm into the 50s and 60s resulting in the atmosphere becoming very unstable. A few thunderstorms developed along the front and merged into a small MCS.  The thunderstorms contained frequent lightning along with heavy rain that quickly turned to heavy snow, graupel and small hail for about a 45 minute period as temperatures quickly dropped into the 30s. In addition, sustained winds of 40 mph along with gusts of 60 mph to around 70 mph resulted in some property damage. Billings reported numerous power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm wind gust of 72 mph was recorded along the Montana/Wyoming border near Ranchester.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extended period of extreme cold developed over western Montana in early December. It was the coldest air mass in more than 4 years to affect the region. A secondary push of arctic air, much colder than the first, moved into the region late Thursday, Dec. 5 and Dec. 6, which helped to bring gusty winds, and very low wind chills to canyon and mountain gap locations. Flathead Lake and nearby shores saw lake effect snow activity develop on Dec. 4 and continue through Dec. 8, bringing anywhere from a trace up to 10 inches of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Pistol Creek RAWS at 6,043 ft., reported wind chill values between -20 and -39 F while wind speeds averaged around 7 mph. It measured the -39 F reading at 12:41 am MST on Dec. 7 with a speed of 11 mph. The Greenough Hill MT DOT sensor measured air temperatures from -20 to -22 F between 5 am and 9:08 am MST on Dec. 7. It also reported an air temperature of -20 F between 11:08 pm Dec. 7 and 2:32 am MST on Dec. 8. The Snowbowl ski resort cancelled their opening day, Friday, Dec. 6, and also Saturday, Dec. 7, due to the extreme cold.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Red Shale Fire began at 4:30 p.m. on July 18th. The fire burned much of the heavy downed and standing dead timber and pockets of thick young lodge pole pines regenerated from the 1988 Gates Park Fire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Red Shale Fire was reported by Beartop Lookout at 4:30 p.m. on July 18th. The fire remains within the 1988 Gates Park Fire perimeter. It had worked its way up Frazier Creek and Route Creek where it crossed to the east of the North Fork Sun River in the Bob Marshall Wilderness. The fire reduced much of the heavy downed and standing dead timber and pockets of thick young lodge pole pines regenerated from the 1988 Gates Park Fire.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Red Shale Fire was reported by Beartop Lookout at 4:30 p.m. on July 18th. The fire remained within the 1988 Gates Park Fire perimeter. It had worked its way up Frazier Creek and Route Creek where it crossed to the east of the North Fork Sun River in the Bob Marshall Wilderness. The fire reduced much of the heavy downed and standing dead timber and pockets of thick young lodge pole pines regenerated from the 1988 Gates Park Fire. The Red Shale Fire encompassed a total of 12,379 acres and was declared out and all closures lifted on October 21 2013.

Blizzard conditions were experienced near the border with North Dakota and winter storm conditions were experienced elsewhere.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cooperative observers reported 10 to 12 inches of new snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level wave moved into Washington State providing the necessary ingredients for a southwest flow severe thunderstorm event to setup. Very strong wind shear combined with meager instability and dew points in the 50s across north-central Idaho allowed for storms to develop in the afternoon. A few of these storms developed into supercells, one of which brought large hail and gusty winds to the Bitterroot Valley.  A very strong cold front moved across Idaho into Western Montana by the evening hours providing gusty winds between 40 and 56 mph which brought down a few trees and power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Agrimet station in Creston reported wind gusts over 50 mph for a 15 minute period and a temperature drop from 72 to 56 degrees as a strong cold front moved through. There were multiple reports of fallen trees including a Ponderosa Pine with a diameter of 40 inches. Power outages were reported in the Evergreen area due to downed power lines.

An intense thunderstorm near Florence produced a strong outflow that traveled north into the Missoula Valley causing multiple trees to be uprooted, downed power lines, & damage to a tent at the Western Montana Fair. The Missoula 911 dispatch reported at least 13 calls related to either power outages or lines down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 170 kt upper level jet dove south out of British Columbia into Washington and Oregon by early Saturday November 16th which provided strong lift and precipitation across north-central Idaho into western Montana. An arctic cold front brought gusty winds and heavy snow at times across Glacier National park and along US-2 from West Glacier to Marias Pass on the 16th.  As the orientation of the upper level jet became more northwesterly by the 17th and moved off to the north and east, showers were felt across much of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weather spotter that works as a ranger for Glacier National Park reported that up to 2 feet of snow had fallen on North Fork Rd., north of the park headquarters. The West Glacier COOP reported 9 of total storm snowfall while the Hungry Horse Dam COOP reported 11. The park ranger reported that several vehicles became stuck in the snow because of the snow depth. The Flattop Mountain SNOTEL, located 9.7 miles northwest of Logan Pass at 6,300 feet reported at storm total snowfall of 16 with 2.00 of total precipitation. The Emery Creek SNOTEL, located 5 miles south-southeast of West Glacier at 4,350 ft., reported 14 of total storm snowfall. A weather spotter from the Spotted Bear ranger station, located 40 miles southeast of Hungry Horse at 3,753 ft., reported a storm total snowfall of 8.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of high pressure helped to strengthen temperature inversions across northwest Montana by the end of November. This allowed for cold pools to develop while temperatures in the higher elevations were able to warm into the 30's and even 40's in a few locations. The upper level flow became more northwesterly allowing for Pacific moisture to sneak around the ridge by Nov. 29th. This created a setup for freezing rain which caused slick roads, and other surfaces including sidewalks across northwest Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weather spotter in Troy reported freezing rain. They believed that the ice was forming on nearby roads making for slick conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two relatively mild and moist upper level waves dropped out of Canada, and brought snow and slick roads to western Montana. When the snow started, temperatures ranged from sub-zero single digits, to above zero single digits Fahrenheit. Road chemicals were not able to mitigate road surfaces at these temperatures, therefore resulting in very slick road conditions until temperatures were able to warm into the 20's to near freezing later on Dec. 10. There were multiple accidents reported, several included injuries, and one resulted in a fatality. The second upper level wave contained more moisture and the atmosphere was a little milder, allowing for larger snow crystal growth and higher snowfall amounts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Seeley Lake RS COOP reported a three day storm total snowfall of 6.9 with 0.32 of liquid equivalent. The Kraft Creek SNOTEL, located 8 miles SSW of Condon measured 13 of snow by 7 am MST on Dec. 10.  There were multiple accidents and slide-offs reported during the storm. There was an injury reported with a crash that happened at 3:43 pm MST on Dec. 9, at the cross streets of MT-200 and Potomac Rd. Road conditions were snow-covered and icy at the time of the crash.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level wave dropped out of British Columbia, embedded in northwesterly flow and brought periods of heavy snow to western Montana. A plume of moisture with tropical origins interacted with this wave, to provide higher snowfall amounts as compared with other northwest waves. Driving conditions were reported to be difficult at times especially along Interstate 90 from the Idaho border to just east of Missoula. Snowfall amounts ranged from 4 to 8 inches in the Valleys and up to a foot in the mountains of western Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A person from the public in Eureka reported 5 of total snowfall with this event. A person from 5 miles north of Fortine measured 4.5 of total snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A colder than normal mid and upper level low moved from western Washington early on September 25th to southern Idaho before it split into two separate systems. One moved to the north and east into eastern Montana while the other sagged south into Utah by the 26th. On the northern periphery of this system in elevations above 5,000 feet, up to 18 inches of snow fell, creating travel impacts, power outages and vegetation damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A person from the public that lives on the southeast side of Georgetown Lake at an elevation of 6,400 feet measured 10 inches of snow with trees down on the south side of the lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Bands of snow showers developed across the region due to a fairly strong upper level northwest jet and unstable conditions from Tuesday November 5th through early Wednesday November 6th. Anywhere from 1 to 6 inches of snow fell with these showers and caused roads to be slick leading to multiple vehicular accidents across western and northwestern Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Seeley Lake HADS sensor at 4,100 ft. reported 0.23 of total precipitation with temperatures around 29 degrees F between Nov. 5 and 6. A weather spotter located 1.6 miles east of Seeley Lake reported 2.8 of snowfall. They reported that the roads were slushy. The Greenough Hill MTDOT sensor reported road temperatures at freezing from 7 pm MST Nov 5 through 8:33 am MST on Nov 6. Other snowfall totals include: 6 1 mi. south of Condon, 5 Kraft Creek SNOTEL 7.5 mi. southwest of Condon, 5 Potomac, and 1.5 Bonner. The Missoula County dispatch reported a vehicle accident near mile marker 107 on I-90 east of Missoula on the morning of Nov. 6th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Bands of snow showers developed across the region due to a fairly strong upper level northwest jet and unstable conditions from Tuesday November 5th through early Wednesday November 6th. Anywhere from 1 to 6 inches of snow fell with these showers and caused roads to be slick leading to multiple vehicular accidents across western and northwestern Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow developed by 1:40 pm MST at Lolo Pass and by 5:30 pm MST at Lost Trail Pass. The snow ended around 7:30 am MST on November 6th. The highway sensor at Lolo Pass reported slick conditions from 3:38 pm MST on 11/5 through 8:24 am MST on 11/6. The highway sensor at Lost Trail Pass reported slick conditions from 8:30 pm MST on 11/5 through 6:30 am MST on 11/6. The Lolo Pass SNOTEL reported 3 of storm snowfall while the Saddle Mountain SNOTEL near Lost Trail received 2 of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of high pressure helped to strengthen temperature inversions across northwest Montana by the end of November. This allowed for cold pools to develop while temperatures in the higher elevations were able to warm into the 30's and even 40's in a few locations. The upper level flow became more northwesterly allowing for Pacific moisture to sneak around the ridge by Nov. 29th. This created a setup for freezing rain which caused slick roads, and other surfaces including sidewalks across northwest Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The trained weather spotter one mile south of Condon reported 1/8th of an inch of ice from freezing rain Friday evening, November 29 through Saturday morning, November 30.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two relatively mild and moist upper level waves dropped out of Canada, and brought snow and slick roads to western Montana. When the snow started, temperatures ranged from sub-zero single digits, to above zero single digits Fahrenheit. Road chemicals were not able to mitigate road surfaces at these temperatures, therefore resulting in very slick road conditions until temperatures were able to warm into the 20's to near freezing later on Dec. 10. There were multiple accidents reported, several included injuries, and one resulted in a fatality. The second upper level wave contained more moisture and the atmosphere was a little milder, allowing for larger snow crystal growth and higher snowfall amounts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Flathead and Mission Valleys saw the highest amount of snowfall in all of western Montana with this storm. Glacier Park International Airport reported a storm total snowfall of 10 with 0.41 liquid equivalent, while the ASOS reported mainly light snow intensities throughout the storm. A head-on automobile accident occurred near Moiese and the National Bison Range on MT Hwy-212 around 11 am MST on Dec 9. A 60 year old woman died as she lost control of her vehicle, and slid into the westbound lane striking an oncoming pickup truck injuring two people. Law enforcement reported that roads were icy at the time of the accident. A 5 vehicle accident occurred on US-93 near the Ninepipes Wildlife Refuge on Dec. 10. Law enforcement blamed the driver of a small U-Haul truck, for not stopping in time and rear-ending three other vehicles. Minor injuries were reported while road conditions were snow-covered and slick at the time of the accident. Several schools closed due to the weather on Dec. 10, including Salish Kootenai College located in Pablo, Polson, and Charlo schools. Two bus routes in the Ronan school district were canceled although schools stayed open. Other total snowfall amounts include: 13 Lake Blaine, 11.5 Polson Kerr DAM COOP, 11 4 SW Fort Connah, St. Ignatius, Creston and Columbia Falls, 10 Whitefish, and 8 at COOP station 13 S Bigfork.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A colder than normal mid and upper level low moved from western Washington early on September 25th to southern Idaho before it split into two separate systems. One moved to the north and east into eastern Montana while the other sagged south into Utah by the 26th. On the northern periphery of this system in elevations above 5,000 feet, up to 18 inches of snow fell, creating travel impacts, power outages and vegetation damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Basin Creek SNOTEL located at 7,180 ft. reported 14 inches of snow with 1.70 of snow water equivalent. The temperatures ranged from freezing to the middle 30s at this station located about 14 miles south of Butte.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A colder than normal mid and upper level low moved from western Washington early on September 25th to southern Idaho before it split into two separate systems. One moved to the north and east into eastern Montana while the other sagged south into Utah by the 26th. On the northern periphery of this system in elevations above 5,000 feet, up to 18 inches of snow fell, creating travel impacts, power outages and vegetation damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter located 7 miles south of Butte at 6,200 ft. estimated greater than 6 inches of heavy snow and measured 1.73 inches of precipitation during this event. They reported that the weight of the snow collapsed their carport and at least 6 aspen trees fell over. They were not home during the event, but at 6 am MDT on September 27th they measured 6 inches.  Using the Basin Creek 8:1 snow to liquid ratio for the event, this spotter could have potentially seen 10 to 14 inches total.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The MacDonald Pass weather station reported moderate to heavy snow between 9 pm MDT on the 25th and 12 am MDT on the 26th.  Road temperatures cooled to freezing at 9 pm and didn't warm up until 9 am MDT on the 26th. Montana DOT reported that chains were required on towing units and also that a semi-truck jack-knifed between 2 and 2:30 am MDT on Highway 12 near MacDonald Pass mile-marker 30 westbound.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: MacDonald Pass on US-12 at milepost 27.9 reported snow from the evening of Nov. 5th through the morning hours of Nov. 6th. Winds were steady 15 to 21 mph with gusts up to 33 mph. Visibility data is not available, but one web cam image from 7 am MST on Nov. 6th indicated blowing snow reducing visibility less than one mile while the roads appeared snow-covered. Media reported 2 inches of snowfall in Bearmouth and 3 inches by Divide (I-15 southwest of Butte).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Bands of snow showers developed across the region due to a fairly strong upper level northwest jet and unstable conditions from Tuesday November 5th through early Wednesday November 6th.
