EVENT_NARRATIVE: Showers brought 1 to 3 inches of snow from Tuesday November 5th through November 6th causing roadways to become slick. There were 7 vehicle accidents reported in Missoula between 6 pm MST Nov. 5th through 3 am MST Nov. 6th. Several of these wrecks occurred on the bridges along Reserve St. with no significant injuries reported. A NWS employee reported snow covered and slick roadways on US-93 from Florence north to Missoula between 5 and 7 am MST on November 6th. Snowfall totals include: 1.5 Missoula Int. Airport, 2 Missoula, 2.5 2 mi. northeast of Missoula, 3 Florence, Trace from Stevensville to Hamilton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An incoming upper level trough brought warm front-type precipitation including snow and freezing rain by early Thursday November 7th. As the freezing level rose to as high as 6,500 ft. MSL by the afternoon on the 7th, those valley locations experiencing frozen precipitation saw a change to rain. Strong westerly winds behind the cold front Thursday evening were able mix down to the surface in the Bitterroot Valley and in the Anaconda area by the evening hours on Nov. 7th causing damage to trees and power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began just before rush hour on Thursday November 7th across portions of Lincoln County. MTDOT at Crystal Creek, about 20 miles south of Libby on US-2, reported an estimated 2.5 of snow before changing to snow by later Thursday morning. A weather spotter located 8 mi. north of Eureka reported 1.5 of snow. At 6:50 am MST on Nov. 7th, MTDOT reported that US-2 from Marion to Libby was slick and MT-56 was snow-packed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist 100 knot southwesterly jet brought moderate snowfall amounts to valley locations of western Montana. Travel became treacherous across area roadways during the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter 2 miles southwest of Newcomb reported a snow storm total of 5.3 inches with 1 to 2 foot snow drifts. They reported blowing snow with gusts to 30 mph with visibilities down to one quarter mile.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist 100 knot southwesterly jet brought moderate snowfall amounts to valley locations of western Montana. Travel became treacherous across area roadways during the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter at Georgetown Lake reported 6 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system from the Gulf of Alaska moved towards the Rocky Mountain Front as jet stream winds over 100 mph moved into the region. The low pressure system and a sharp cold front reached North-Central Montana Wednesday, adding moisture and light snow to the mix.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High sustained winds measuring 35KT (40MPH) were reported 2 mi SSE of Geyser, MT at the Geyser DOT site.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The MTDOT section between Kalispell and Whitefish, reported a layer of glaze that caused road surfaces to be slick during the early morning hours from 4 to 7 am MST on Nov. 29. A weather spotter from Columbia Falls reported freezing drizzle during the morning of Nov. 29 precipitating out of fog.  This spotter overheard multiple reports of vehicles spinning out due to the slick roads. A weather spotter located just west of Kalispell reported that mist developed during the early morning hours of Nov. 29 that caused sidewalks to become slick.  Light snow fell from 4 to 6:30 pm MST on Nov. 29 in the Kalispell area. The Flathead County 911 dispatch indicated that the combination of snow on top of icy conditions caused a few accidents and slide-offs. The Lake County 911 dispatch reported icy roads at Terrace Lake in the Ronan area at 9 am MST on Nov. 30. A spotter 8 mi. northwest of St. Ignatius reported freezing rain that caused the sidewalks to become icy during the late morning hours of Nov. 30. At 11:01 pm MST on Nov. 30, a spotter located in Columbia Falls tweeted that roads were solid sheets of ice due to freezing rain and were treacherous. They reportedly drove down East Tamarack Hill sideways.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low amplitude upper ridge built into the region between Saturday Nov. 9th and Monday Nov. 11th. A plume of subtropical moisture brought steady precipitation and pushed  freezing levels up to 8,000 ft. across northwest Montana. A modified arctic air mass dropped out of Alberta, Canada during the early morning hours of Sunday Nov. 10th and it had just enough energy to push westward over the Continental Divide affecting the US-2 corridor from West Glacier to Marias Pass. Over a foot of snow was reported in these areas causing high impacts to travel.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported that road conditions were terrible at 7:22 am MST Nov. 10th from West Glacier to Columbia Falls on US-2. The West Glacier COOP reported a storm total snowfall of 12 from Nov. 10-11 with 0.97 of precipitation. They also reported 0.67 of precipitation on Nov. 10th which was the 2nd highest amount since 1949. Other storm total snowfall amounts include: 7 Hungry Horse, 8-10 in Polebridge, and 7 Noisy Basin SNOTEL in the Swan Range at 6,040 ft.

The Deer Lodge County sheriff dispatch reported several trees and power lines down on the west side of Anaconda. A tree fell onto a power line near the intersection of Mt. Haggin Dr. and Theater Lane and started a fire. This location is approximately 2.5 miles west-northwest of Anaconda. There was a power outage which lasted for 2 hours and 20 minutes.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The West Glacier COOP observer reported 2.7 of snowfall with 0.71 of precipitation for November 7th. The MTDOT road sensor at Essex reported steady snow for much of the day on the 7th and was intermittent on the 8th. The web cam at Essex depicted snow-covered conditions at 10:46 am on the 7th. The only period that saw above freezing road surface temperatures on US-2 at Essex was between 1 pm and 5:28 pm MST on the 7th.  Flattop Mountain SNOTEL (6,300 ft.), located 20 miles east-northeast of Polebridge reported a storm total precipitation of 1.30. The Pike Creek SNOTEL (5,930 ft.), located near Marias Pass reported 6 of snow from 6 pm MST on the 7th to 5 pm MST on the 8th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 170 kt upper level jet dove south out of British Columbia into Washington and Oregon by early Saturday November 16th which provided strong lift and precipitation across north-central Idaho into western Montana. An arctic cold front brought gusty winds and heavy snow at times across Glacier National park and along US-2 from West Glacier to Marias Pass on the 16th.  As the orientation of the upper level jet became more northwesterly by the 17th and moved off to the north and east, showers were felt across much of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: MTDOT reported up 3 to 5 inches of storm total snowfall on US-2 between Libby and Happy's Inn with up to 8 inches that fell along a 5 mile stretch of US-2 between McGinnis Meadows to Loon Lake in a 36 hour period. This caused slick conditions at times due to snow and slush on the roads. The road sensor at Crystal Creek, about 19.6 miles south-southeast of Libby, reported snow with road temperatures at or below freezing during the evening and morning hours between the 15th and 17th. Other snow totals include: 4.8 9 mi. NNE of Yaak COOP, 6.1 30 mi. SSE of Libby COOP, 1.5 Libby 1 mi. NE RS COOP, 8 Olney COOP, and 2.9 1 mi. SE Marion.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP observer located in Olney reported 8 of total snowfall from Nov. 16 through Nov. 18. A person from the public reported that the falling snow on Nov. 16, was very heavy and dense with difficult driving conditions in the Olney area. The Hand Creek SNOTEL at 5,035 ft., located 20 miles southwest of Olney reported 16 of snowfall and 1.50 of precipitation from Nov 15-17. This sensor also reported periods of heavy snow during the morning and afternoon hours on the 16th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Cody, Wyoming man lost his life in a fatal wreck on U. S. Highway 310 in Carbon County. Montana Highway Patrol reported the 51-year-old man was driving a pickup northbound in snowy conditions. His truck fish-tailed into the other lane, hitting a semi trailer coming southbound.

The one-quarter mile visibility was observed from 8:55 am MST through 2:35 pm MST on Nov. 27.

The Pike Creek SNOTEL located 1.6 miles southeast of Marias Pass reported a total of 8 hours of heavy snow between 4 am and 5 pm MST on the 5th with a storm total snowfall of 11. The arctic front pushed through Marias Pass by 5:30 am MST on Nov. 10th which contributed to the heavy snowfall rates. The Shed 4 weather station (4,520 ft.) located 6 mi. southwest of Marias Pass measured 14.8 of snow with 0.75 of precipitation. The Summit HADS located at Marias Pass measured 0.60 of precipitation which could equate to 12 to 14 of snow(using a 20 to 1 snow to liquid ratio which matches Shed 4 observations).

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Glacier Park International Airport reported 1 of snowfall with 0.40 of precipitation between Nov. 7-8. With ground temperatures at or just below freezing on the morning of November 7th, roads became slick with the light snowfall. There were 6 vehicle accidents and 4 slideoffs reported in the Flathead Valley from 7 am to 9:32 am MST on Nov. 7th. The Flathead County dispatch reported that many of the accidents were as a result of the slick conditions caused by the snow. The MTDOT road reports at 8:47 am MST on Nov. 7th indicated that MT-35 from Yellow Bay to S-206 Jct., and Kalispell to Whitefish had snowcover.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An incoming upper level trough brought warm front-type precipitation including snow and freezing rain by early Thursday November 7th. As the freezing level rose to as high as 6,500 ft. MSL by the afternoon on the 7th, those valley locations experiencing frozen precipitation saw a change to rain. Strong westerly winds behind the cold front Thursday evening were able mix down to the surface in the Bitterroot Valley and in the Anaconda area by the evening hours on Nov. 7th causing damage to trees and power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: MTDOT reported freezing rain from Swamp Creek Hill (mile-marker 24 on MT-200) or 1 mile east-southeast of Tuscor at 6:50 am MST on Nov. 7th. On Nov. 7th, Lookout Pass on I-90 saw snow briefly change to freezing rain by 9:27 am MST before changing to all rain by 10 am.  Lookout Pass saw a change back to snow by 4:38 pm MST on the 7th and continued through 11:52 am MST on the 8th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An incoming upper level trough brought warm front-type precipitation including snow and freezing rain by early Thursday November 7th. EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ITD Lolo Pass sensor reported snow that changed to freezing rain by 5:38 am MST which continued until 9:40 am on Nov. 7th. When the cold front moved through around 7 pm, it reported heavy snow which changed to light snow by 7:53 pm. The Lolo Pass SNOTEL reported a precipitation total of 1.20, but its snow depth sensor was not working properly to be able to determine snowfall totals. Of that precipitation total, 0.50 fell after the cold front went through, so at least 5 of snowfall is possible at Lolo Pass. Lost Trail Pass reported periods of snow starting at 5:30 am MST and continued through 4:30 am MST on the 8th. The ITD sensor reported dense fog between 1:30 and 7:30 pm MST on the 7th. The Saddle Mountain SNOTEL located 1 mile west of Lost Trail reported a storm total snowfall of 6 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong and moist westerly flow brought rain and snow to the mountains of North-Central and Southwest Montana. A Winter Storm Warning was issued for the mountains of Central and Southwest Montana (above 5000 feet across North-Central/Central Montana - above 6000 feet in Southwest Montana). Forecast models indicated likely snow totals through the period of 8 to 18 inches, with up to 2 feet at the higher peaks. Gusty westerly winds shifted northerly with the frontal passage on Monday, keeping blowing snow and reduced visibility across the Rocky Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow measuring 8.0 inches in 24 hours reported at the Pike Creek SNOTEL; elevation 5928 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level wave dropped out of British Columbia, embedded in northwesterly flow and brought periods of heavy snow to western Montana. A plume of moisture with tropical origins interacted with this wave, to provide higher snowfall amounts as compared with other northwest waves. Driving conditions were reported to be difficult at times especially along Interstate 90 from the Idaho border to just east of Missoula. Snowfall amounts ranged from 4 to 8 inches in the Valleys and up to a foot in the mountains of western Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow was reported by MT DOT for I-90, especially from Alberton to Frenchtown and snow plows could barely keep up. The MT DOT also reported that US-93 at Evaro Hill experienced heavy snow for several hours during the afternoon and evening of Dec. 20. A weather spotter located in Alberton reported that conditions were severe on I-90, especially in the passing lane at 8:30 MST on Dec. 20. Snowfall amounts: 8 Evaro Hill, 6.7 Huson, 6.3 Frenchtown, and 5 lower Evaro Hill.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall was not as heavy in terms of sensible weather during this event, but because the timing occurred during high volume traffic, it became a high impact event. Steady snowfall continued during rush hour traffic on a weekend preceding the holidays, and this caused more people on the roads traveling to holiday parties etc. At 6:31 pm MST, the Missoula Sheriff's office issued an emergency alert asking people to stay off the roads, so that snow plows could efficiently mitigate poor road conditions. Several accidents were reported in the City of Missoula and along US-93 south to Victor.  In the immediate Missoula Valley, snowfall amounts ranged from 4.7 at the Missoula International Airport to 6 on the South Hills of Missoula. In the Bitterroot Valley from Lolo to Stevensville, snowfall amounts ranged from 6 to 8.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds measuring 52KT (60MPH) were reported 2 mi E of Inverness, MT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For the 3rd time in the 2013/2014 season, an arctic cold front clipped western Montana. Initially, Pacific moisture allowed for light to moderate snow to develop across western Montana, with many higher elevation locations receiving several inches.  Valleys saw temperatures warm into the lower 40's F with rain showers early on Dec. 18. These milder temperatures set the stage for a flash-freezing event in the Flathead Valley east to Marias Pass when an arctic air-mass moved in by the late evening of Dec. 18. As the arctic air-mass pushed westward, gusty winds to 50 mph in the canyons caused blowing snow, while areas from West Glacier to Marias Pass saw heavy snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: MT DOT estimated that 8 to 10 inches of snow fell from West Glacier to Java East, which is located about 6 miles southwest of Marias Pass. They also reported that there was blowing and drifting snow, snow-pack and ice on US-2. They also indicated that the area south of Essex to Java East was particularly nasty. The Pike Creek SNOTEL at 5,930 ft., located about 1.6 miles southeast of Marias Pass, reported 6 of total snow with 0.20 of snow water equivalent, which gives an average snow to liquid ratio of 30 to 1. The HADS sensor at Marias Pass, reported 0.29 of liquid, which could equate to around 7.1 of snow, 6 of that fell between 4:45 and 7:45 pm MST on Dec. 18. The MT DOT sensor located at milepost 179.9 in Essex, reported gusty winds near 30 mph from 4 to 7:30 pm MST on Dec. 18, which corroborates the report of nasty conditions on US-2. An automobile accident with an injury was reported near West glacier at 4:24 pm MST on Dec. 18, while roads were reported to have snow-cover with slick conditions. Blowing snow with wind gusts near 50 mph were reported near Bad Rock canyon on the evening of Dec. 18. Other snow/liquid amounts: 7.0/0.20 Flattop Mtn. SNOTEL, 7.0/0.40 Emery Creek SNOTEL(5 SSE of West Glacier) & 4.4/0.41 West Glacier COOP.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For the second time during the 2013/2014 winter season, a strong arctic cold front plowed south and west across western Montana. The initial push of arctic air undercut moist Pacific air causing heavy snow, blowing snow and poor visibility. This arctic outbreak was very similar to those that occurred in early December of 1972 and also in 1956.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A railroad weather sensor at 4,520 ft., located 6.4 miles southwest of Marias Pass, measured 2 per hour snowfall rates between 6 and 8 am MST on Dec. 2. It also depicted a distinct wind shift with falling temperatures between 9 and 10 am MST, associated with an arctic cold front passage. It measured a total storm snowfall of 5.5 inches. A person from the public located in Essex, estimated that 12 inches of total snow fell during this event. They also reported that 6 inches of that fell as heavy snow with and behind the arctic front passage near 9:30 am MST. At 1 pm MST, the MT DOT reported that chains were required on towing units on US-2 from Essex to 7 miles west of Marias Pass.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Saddle Mountain SNOTEL at 7,900 ft. located about 1.7 miles west-southwest of Lost Trail Pass on US-93 received 27 inches of total storm snow with 3.50 inches of snow water equivalent. An intense, 4 hour period of heavy snow amounting to 9 inches fell between 4 and 8 pm MST on Dec. 1. At 1:40 pm MST Dec. 1, the Montana DOT reported that chains were required on towing units for Lost Trail Pass. The heaviest snowfall, according to the SNOTEL, appeared to have stopped by 2 pm MST on Dec. 2, but periods of light to moderate snow continued through the morning hours of Dec. 3.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure over southern Saskatchewan kept North-Central and Southwest Montana under an increasingly cold northerly flow. Winds were generally light across the region, but 10 mph across most of North-Central Montana and portions of Southwest Montana, combined with temperatures in the teens below zero, caused widespread wind chill values of 25 below to 45 below zero during overnight hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chill value of 44 below zero reported 7 mi S of Neihart, MT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 170 kt upper level jet dove south out of British Columbia into Washington and Oregon by early Saturday November 16th which provided strong lift and precipitation across north-central Idaho into western Montana. An arctic cold front brought gusty winds and heavy snow at times across Glacier National park and along US-2 from West Glacier to Marias Pass on the 16th.  As the orientation of the upper level jet became more northwesterly by the 17th and moved off to the north and east, showers were felt across much of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Poorman Creek SNOTEL at 5,100 ft., located 18 miles south-southwest of Libby in the Cabinet Mountains, reported 18 of snowfall from Nov. 15-17. It reported heavy snow from 1 pm to 10 pm MST on Nov. 16 and from 10 am to 1 pm MST on Nov. 17. This  created dangerous conditions for hunters out in the higher terrain.

A weather spotter reported 11.5 of total storm snowfall with 8 of that falling on the evening of the 15th through the morning of the 16th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two relatively mild and moist upper level waves dropped out of Canada, and brought snow and slick roads to western Montana. When the snow started, temperatures ranged from sub-zero single digits, to above zero single digits Fahrenheit. Road chemicals were not able to mitigate road surfaces at these temperatures, therefore resulting in very slick road conditions until temperatures were able to warm into the 20's to near freezing later on Dec. 10. There were multiple accidents reported, several included injuries, and one resulted in a fatality. The second upper level wave contained more moisture and the atmosphere was a little milder, allowing for larger snow crystal growth and higher snowfall amounts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A person from the public in Olney tweeted that they received 11 of fluffy snow as of 11:59 am MST on Dec. 10.  At 6:40 pm MST, they commented that US-93 near mile-marker 145 was plowed but slick with ice and snow. Other storm snowfall amounts COOP stations include: 1.9 Yaak 9 NNE, 1.8 Libby 30 SSE, 2.5 Libby 1 NE, and 2.5 at the Eureka Ranger Station. The common 24 hour snow to liquid ratios, as observed by these COOP stations, ranged between 20 and 30 to one.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another plume of Pacific moisture and relatively milder air moved over the cold valleys of western Montana on December 1st.  This brought wet snow to area mountain passes while freezing rain and drizzle made for slick driving conditions to a few valley locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Saddle Mountain SNOTEL at 7,900 ft., located about 1.7 miles west-southwest of Lost Trail Pass on US-93 received 15 inches of snow with 1.80 inches of snow water equivalent. An intense period of heavy snow occurred between 4 and 8 pm MST when 9 inches fell. At 1:40 pm MST, the Montana DOT reported that chains were required on towing units for Lost Trail Pass. During the morning hours, freezing rain was reported near Sula on US-93 causing very slick conditions. Several vehicles had slid off the road from Sula towards Lost Trail Pass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The seasons first arctic outbreak and combined winter storm moved into the Billings Forecast Area during the first week of December. Mild temperatures ended the month of November and continued through the morning of the 2nd. A strong disturbance moved across the area during the afternoon of the 2nd. This disturbance combined with temperatures around 50 degrees for most of the day, resulted in a period of light to moderate rain for a few hours. An arctic front moved into the area by late in the afternoon turning the rain to snow. Temperatures quickly dropped into the teens and single digits during the overnight hours. The initial wet snow that fell onto already wet pavement from the rain quickly froze which resulted in very icy road conditions. The snow, which was heavy at times, continued through the 3rd before ending on the 4th of December.  However, bitterly cold temperatures continued through the 8th with daytime highs only in the single digits above and below zero with overnight lows in the teens below to 30s below zero.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 12 to 18 inches was common across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An incoming upper level trough brought warm front-type precipitation including snow and freezing rain by early Thursday November 7th. As the freezing level rose to as high as 6,500 ft. MSL by the afternoon on the 7th, those valley locations experiencing frozen precipitation saw a change to rain. Strong westerly winds behind the cold front Thursday evening were able mix down to the surface in the Bitterroot Valley and in the Anaconda area by the evening hours on Nov. 7th causing damage to trees and power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A cold front brought rain that changed to snow with strong gusty winds at MacDonald Pass on US-12 at milepost 27.9 around 9:14 pm MST on November 7th. Winds gusted as high as 42 mph with the frontal passage, but continued to be blustery between 20 and 41 mph through the night into the morning of November 8th. With the combination of relative humidity levels at one hundred percent and falling snow with strong winds, the visibility could have been near zero from 9:14 to 10:46 pm MST on Nov. 7th. Blowing snow was not an impact due to the wet snow, but road conditions remained slick through the morning of the 8th. Other locations affected by the cold front include Georgetown Lake, Butte and Homestake Pass. These areas saw light accumulation of snow on the road with strong gusty winds between 30 and 40 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For the second time during the 2013/2014 winter season, a strong arctic cold front plowed south and west across western Montana. The initial push of arctic air undercut moist Pacific air causing heavy snow, blowing snow and poor visibility. This arctic outbreak was very similar to those that occurred in early December of 1972 and also in 1956.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Montana DOT reported heavy snow with snow-covered roads on MT-200 from Trout Creek to west of Plains at 6:18 pm MST on Dec. 2. By 7 pm MST, they reported heavy snow on MT-28 junction with MT-200 to 10 miles north. The MT DOT also reported heavy snow on US-93 at Evaro Hill. Rain had fallen earlier in the day on the 2nd and when the arctic front went through by 6 pm MST at Trout Creek, snow froze quickly to the road making for very slick conditions.  On Dec. 3, the MT DOT indicated that from 5:23 am to 7:11 am MST, there were severe driving conditions on MT-200 from Trout Creek to just west of Plains due to snow-pack and black ice.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For the second time during the 2013/2014 winter season, a strong arctic cold front plowed south and west across western Montana. The initial push of arctic air undercut moist Pacific air causing heavy snow, blowing snow and poor visibility. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter located 2 miles west-southwest of Columbia Falls reported visibilities near zero from at least 1 to 3 pm MST due to blowing snow. During this time-frame the Glacier Park International Airport ASOS sensor reported frequent gusts between 32 and 38 mph. Based on this, wind speeds could have been reaching near 50 mph towards the Bad Rock Canyon on US-2. The Flathead County 911 dispatch reported that 15 vehicle slide-offs and accidents occurred during a time span of less than two hours inside and outside of the Kalispell city limits, some of which contained injuries. The Flathead Beacon tweeted at 3:31 pm MST, Terrible driving conditions currently-please drive with caution. Even though snowfall amounts were low and near 2 inches, the sudden change from dry roads to near whiteout conditions with dropping temperatures caused high impact to the Flathead Valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level wave dropped out of British Columbia, embedded in northwesterly flow and brought periods of heavy snow to western Montana. A plume of moisture with tropical origins interacted with this wave, to provide higher snowfall amounts as compared with other northwest waves. Driving conditions were reported to be difficult at times especially along Interstate 90 from the Idaho border to just east of Missoula. Snowfall amounts ranged from 4 to 8 inches in the Valleys and up to a foot in the mountains of western Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Glacier Park International Airport reported 5.4 of snow with 0.21 of liquid equivalent. A weather spotter in Columbia Falls tweeted that the powdery snow had fallen over icy roads, which made for slick driving at 12:26 pm MST and at 4:07 pm MST. This spotter measured a total of 8 of snow at 11:30 pm MST and reported that secondary roads had deep snow. Someone from Kalispell tweeted at 2:50 pm MST that the roads in Kalispell were nasty and suggested that people stay home. At 10:30 pm, a weather spotter located 2 miles NNW of Kalispell reported 8.3 of snow. The MT road incidents reported 10 automobile crashes with no injuries, 3 crashes with injuries and 8 slide-offs in the Flathead and Mission Valleys on Dec. 20.

Road conditions at the time of the accidents were moderate to heavy snowfall, snow-covered and icy. Several accidents were also reported on Highway 83 from Seeley Lake to Condon. Snowfall reports: 8 Potomac, 7 Condon, 5 in Bonner and 2 in Seeley Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For the 3rd time in the 2013/2014 season, an arctic cold front clipped western Montana. Initially, Pacific moisture allowed for light to moderate snow to develop across western Montana, with many higher elevation locations receiving several inches.  Valleys saw temperatures warm into the lower 40's F with rain showers early on Dec. 18. These milder temperatures set the stage for a flash-freezing event in the Flathead Valley east to Marias Pass when an arctic air-mass moved in by the late evening of Dec. 18. As the arctic air-mass pushed westward, gusty winds to 50 mph in the canyons caused blowing snow, while areas from West Glacier to Marias Pass saw heavy snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter located in Columbia Falls, reported 3 of total snowfall, flash-freezing to formerly wet road surfaces, and blowing snow with occasional strong gusts between 30 and 50 mph. They reported that visibility lowered to less than one-quarter mile at times due to blowing snow during the evening of Dec. 18. They also said that the drive from Whitefish to Columbia Falls around 11:30 pm MST on Dec. 18, was very slick due to ice, blowing and drifting snow. The arctic cold front made it through Glacier Park International Airport around 8:22 pm MST Dec. 18 and north-northeast winds were sustained between 30-33 mph with gusts up to 45 mph through 2:35 am MST Dec. 19. The airport only reported 1 of snow, and visibilities were only reduced to one-half mile between 11:24 and 11:55 pm MST Dec. 18. Five automobile crashes and 7 slide-offs were reported in the Flathead Valley on Dec. 18.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter 7 miles south of Butte at 6,200 ft., reported a total storm snowfall of 3 inches that mainly fell after midnight on Dec. 19. A weather observer at the Bert Mooney Airport also reported an estimated 3 of snowfall during the event. The temperatures ranged from the single digits below zero to the teens in the Butte area through Dec. 20, which prevented the DOT to be able to mitigate the slick roads. Wind chill values ranged between 10 and 16 below zero F. from Avon to Deer Lodge from 4:30 to 10:21 am MST on Dec. 19. A trained spotter located near Georgetown Lake reported 4 of total storm snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For the 3rd time in the 2013/2014 season, an arctic cold front clipped western Montana. Initially, Pacific moisture allowed for light to moderate snow to develop across western Montana, with many higher elevation locations receiving several inches.  Valleys saw temperatures warm into the lower 40's F with rain showers early on Dec. 18. These milder temperatures set the stage for a flash-freezing event in the Flathead Valley east to Marias Pass when an arctic air-mass moved in by the late evening of Dec. 18. As the arctic air-mass pushed westward, gusty winds to 50 mph in the canyons caused blowing snow, while areas from West Glacier to Marias Pass saw heavy snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Olney COOP reported a total storm snowfall of 5 with 0.38 of precipitation for Dec. 19. A person from the public in Olney, tweeted that road conditions were snow-covered and snow intensities were heavy at 3:50 pm MST on Dec. 18.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two relatively mild and moist upper level waves dropped out of Canada, and brought snow and slick roads to western Montana. When the snow started, temperatures ranged from sub-zero single digits, to above zero single digits Fahrenheit. Road chemicals were not able to mitigate road surfaces at these temperatures, therefore resulting in very slick road conditions until temperatures were able to warm into the 20's to near freezing later on Dec. 10. There were multiple accidents reported, several included injuries, and one resulted in a fatality. The second upper level wave contained more moisture and the atmosphere was a little milder, allowing for larger snow crystal growth and higher snowfall amounts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A cooperative observer located one mile west of Haugan, measured a storm total snowfall of 9 with 0.30 of liquid equivalent. The Superior COOP didn't report snow, but did report a liquid equivalent of 0.42, which could have equated to near a foot of snow, using a 30 to 1 snow to liquid ratio. The St. Regis schools closed on Dec. 10 due to the weather. Other snowfall reports include: 10 Thompson Falls, 7 Belknap 2 WNW, 6.4 in Alberton, and 6 Evaro.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous moist Pacific system brought snow to the higher elevations of western Montana impacting mountain passes including Lookout and Lost Trail.  Impacts such as slick roads were reported at pass level by late evening Saturday November 2nd and continued through Monday the 4th. The frontal system brought heavy precipitation (up to 1.60) across the higher elevations of western Montana. Anywhere from 8 to 12 inches of snow were reported in the mountains while valley locations received a trace to 4 inches. A stalled out arctic front brought heavy snow to US-2 from Essex to Marias Pass between Nov. 3 & 4.  A second cold-core upper level trough provided strong unstable conditions that helped to trigger winter waterspouts on Flathead Lake and intense valley-level snow showers on Nov. 4th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An avalanche forecaster measured 16 inches of total storm snowfall in Essex by Monday afternoon Nov. 4th, 12 hours after the snowfall ended. The BNSF Shed 4 weather sensor (4520 ft.) located approximately 10 miles southwest of Marias Pass just off of US-2, reported 12 of snow with 0.90 of precipitation 32 hours ending at 7 am MST on Monday Nov. 4th. It reported heavy snowfall rates between 1 to 1.8 per hour from 1 and 8 pm MST on Sunday Nov. 3rd. The Shed 7 weather station at 6363 ft. and 11.5 miles southwest of Marias Pass, reported 25 of snow from this storm. On Nov. 3rd, the arctic front pushed through Marias Pass by 3 pm MST and into Essex by 5:16 pm MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving north-south oriented cold front with embedded showers and thunderstorms moved west to east at approximately 45 mph across western Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 72 foot, 26 inch diameter Douglas Fir fell down from very strong thunderstorm winds and crashed onto owners' house. Soil moisture was relatively dry, but as seen in photo much of the branches on the trunk except at the top had already been cut off before this event.  A forester examined the tree and found that its roots had been cut on the west side of the tree before the event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Bands of snow showers developed across the region due to a fairly strong upper level northwest jet and unstable conditions from Tuesday November 5th through early Wednesday November 6th. Anywhere from 1 to 6 inches of snow fell with these showers and caused roads to be slick leading to multiple vehicular accidents across western and northwestern Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media on Facebook reported an estimated 3.3 inches of snowfall in Eureka. Temperatures remained at 30 degrees F during the snowfall while roads became slick.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Glacier Park International Airport reported 3 of snowfall through 5 am MST on Nov. 6th. Multiple vehicle slide-offs and accidents were reported in the Kalispell area. Other snow totals from this storm include: Kalispell 1.8, Whitefish 4, Bigfork/Ferndale 3.8, Creston 2.8, Lake Blaine 3.5, 1 mi. SW Columbia Falls 5, 4 mi. southeast Charlo 1.5, and Ronan 2.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 170 kt upper level jet dove south out of British Columbia into Washington and Oregon by early Saturday November 16th which provided strong lift and precipitation across north-central Idaho into western Montana. An arctic cold front brought gusty winds and heavy snow at times across Glacier National park and along US-2 from West Glacier to Marias Pass on the 16th.  As the orientation of the upper level jet became more northwesterly by the 17th and moved off to the north and east, showers were felt across much of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous natural (slab) and human triggered avalanches were reported in the Jewel Basin in the Swan Range by the Flathead Avalanche Center on Saturday November 16th. The Noisy Basin SNOTEL located 8.8 mi. east-southeast of Creston at 6,040 ft. reported a storm total snowfall of 24 with 3.20 of precipitation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extended period of extreme cold developed over western Montana in early December. It was the coldest air mass in more than 4 years to affect the region. A secondary push of arctic air, much colder than the first, moved into the region late Thursday, Dec. 5 and Dec. 6, which helped to bring gusty winds, and very low wind chills to canyon and mountain gap locations. Flathead Lake and nearby shores saw lake effect snow activity develop on Dec. 4 and continue through Dec. 8, bringing anywhere from a trace up to 10 inches of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Glacier Park International Airport ASOS reported wind chill values between -20 and -30 F, while north-northeast winds averaged around 15 mph. Both the City of Columbia Falls and Polson cancelled their Parade of Lights Dec. 6 due to the cold. The Bigfork Area Chamber of Commerce cancelled the Annual Magical Holiday Parade that was planned to occur on Dec. 7.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two relatively mild and moist upper level waves dropped out of Canada, and brought snow and slick roads to western Montana. When the snow started, temperatures ranged from sub-zero single digits, to above zero single digits Fahrenheit. Road chemicals were not able to mitigate road surfaces at these temperatures, therefore resulting in very slick road conditions until temperatures were able to warm into the 20's to near freezing later on Dec. 10. There were multiple accidents reported, several included injuries, and one resulted in a fatality. The second upper level wave contained more moisture and the atmosphere was a little milder, allowing for larger snow crystal growth and higher snowfall amounts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The official Missoula International Airport storm total snowfall measured was 5.8 with 0.19 of liquid during this storm. The snow fell lightly on Dec. 9 and wreaked havoc on area roads causing multiple accidents due to slick conditions. At 11:37 am on Dec. 9, a 2 car crash on US-93 south of Florence left a woman and teenage boy with serious injuries. US-93 was shut down for several hours and authorities said that conditions were very slick at the time when the first car went out of control. At 1:54 pm MST on Dec. 9, a SUV rolled over in the flats, just north of Lolo on US-93, causing 2 people to be injured. Law enforcement indicated that the road was snow covered and icy at the time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A colder than normal mid and upper level low moved from western Washington early on September 25th to southern Idaho before it split into two separate systems. One moved to the north and east into eastern Montana while the other sagged south into Utah by the 26th. On the northern periphery of this system in elevations above 5,000 feet, up to 18 inches of snow fell, creating travel impacts, power outages and vegetation damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A person from the public located 6 miles south-southeast of Janney at an elevation around 7,100 feet, estimated that 18 inches fell with the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Bands of snow showers developed across the region due to a fairly strong upper level northwest jet and unstable conditions from Tuesday November 5th through early Wednesday November 6th. Anywhere from 1 to 6 inches of snow fell with these showers and caused roads to be slick leading to multiple vehicular accidents across western and northwestern Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow developed during the morning of Tuesday November 5th at Lookout Pass on Interstate 90, on the border of Idaho and Montana. It continued through much of the night and ended Wednesday morning. Web cams at the pass depicted slick road conditions especially during the night-time hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 170 kt upper level jet dove south out of British Columbia into Washington and Oregon by early Saturday November 16th which provided strong lift and precipitation across north-central Idaho into western Montana. An arctic cold front brought gusty winds and heavy snow at times across Glacier National park and along US-2 from West Glacier to Marias Pass on the 16th.  As the orientation of the upper level jet became more northwesterly by the 17th and moved off to the north and east, showers were felt across much of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Lolo Pass SNOTEL located at 5,240 ft., reported a total of 19 of snow between Nov. 15-17. It reported 9 of heavy snow between 2 and 10 am MST on Nov. 16 and also from 5 to 7 am on Nov. 17. The ITD road sensor at Lolo Pass reported slick conditions for much of the weekend and only improving by Monday Nov. 18th. The Saddle Mountain SNOTEL at 7,900 ft., located one mile west of Lost Trail Pass reported 11 of total snowfall with 0.60 precipitation between Nov. 15-17. Other snowfall totals include: 14 Twelvemile Creek located 13.2 mi. west of Charlos Height at 5,600 ft. and 22 Twin Lakes SNOTEL located 16 mi. west of Charlos Height at 6,400 ft. The Twin Lakes sensor also reported 2.10 of total precipitation during the event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very cold arctic air mass from the Canadian prairies combined with moisture from the Pacific Ocean.  This was the first winter storm of the season and it impacted areas in and near the Yellowstone River Valley and Big Sheep Mountains.  Snowfall reports ranged from 4 to 10 inches, and with some winds of 15 to 25 mph, drifting and blowing snow reduced visibilities in some areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple reports of snow amounts ranging from 4-8 inches were common in the town of Circle throughout most of the day.  Afternoon winds increased into the 15 to 25 mph range to create some blowing and drifting snow.  Severe driving conditions and near-zero visibility were also reported.

Showers and thunderstorms developed and moved across the plains of North-Central Montana during the afternoon and evening. Precipitation was enhanced by continued low level easterly up-slope flow. Over Southwest Montana, increased instability during the afternoon, coupled with the presence of a jet streak, resulted in scattered thunderstorm development with some storms becoming strong to severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail measuring 1.00 inches (quarter sized) was reported in Stanford, MT.

An unusually warm and moist airmass for early September was in place across Southern Montana and Northern Wyoming. A strong upper level disturbance aloft moved across the area during the late afternoon into the evening hours. As a result, a cluster of very strong to severe thunderstorms developed. Very heavy rain, large hail and strong wind gusts accompanied the thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds pushed 1200 pound round hay bales through two fences.

An upper level trough moved from southwest Oregon on the morning of August 25th to southern British Columbia by the morning of the 26th. This system initiated strong thunderstorms on the evening of the 25th and also during the early morning hours on the 26th. These storms produced gusty winds that caused trees to topple over causing power outages and vehicle damage across portions of northwest Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter from Woods Bay reported power outages with at least one tree snapped in half.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very cold arctic air mass from the Canadian prairies combined with moisture from the Pacific Ocean.  This was the first winter storm of the season and it impacted areas in and near the Yellowstone River Valley and Big Sheep Mountains.  Snowfall reports ranged from 4 to 10 inches, and with some winds of 15 to 25 mph, drifting and blowing snow reduced visibilities in some areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Both the county DES coordinator and a cooperative observer in Sidney reported 10 inches of snow as well as significant road issues in and around the Sidney area.  Winds were in the 15 to 25 mph range.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough moved from southwest Oregon on the morning of August 25th to southern British Columbia by the morning of the 26th. This system initiated strong thunderstorms on the evening of the 25th and also during the early morning hours on the 26th. These storms produced gusty winds that caused trees to topple over causing power outages and vehicle damage across portions of northwest Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Kalispell ASOS station measured a strong southerly wind gust to 55 mph.  The Flathead Valley power companies reported that around 5,000 customers lost power from 8pm MDT through the early morning hours on August 26. Multiple trees fell on power lines causing the outages.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm packing high winds, hail, and torrential rain battered the city of Billings and nearby areas during the evening of the 7th. Almost 2 inches of rain fell in 30-45 minutes causing significant flooding for some residential areas, as well as in the downtown area. Winds gusted to 73 mph at the Billings Airport which resulted in power outages for thousands of people across town. Some power poles were knocked down and numerous large trees were uprooted. In addition, half-dollar size hail occurred just west of Billings. |A large rock blocked Zimmerman Trail and Highway 3 west of Zimmerman Trail had drifts of hail alongside the road. The rain that washed off the Rimrocks pushed debris, including mud and rocks, onto North 27th Street near Montana State University Billings. High water at the corner of First Avenue North and Main Street caused multiple cars to stall on the road. In addition, all of the underpasses in the downtown area were closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 50 to 60 knot mid-level jet maximum associated with an upper level low over central Washington moved into northwest Montana by the mid-afternoon on September 6, 2013. This triggered thunderstorms that mainly brought hail and rain to the Kalispell area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Kalispell International airport tower observer estimated half dollar sized hail. Out of at least 5 vehicles damaged, one cost $5,600 to repair.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper level low over southwestern Oregon brought moisture into western Montana on August 12th. A very weak mid-level wind maximum around 30 knots along with strong surface heating into the lower 90s and the lifting mechanism due to terrain helped to trigger multicellular thunderstorms that tracked into the Seeley Lake region. The main impacts of these storms were lightning, tree and power line damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Missoula 911 dispatch reported multiple trees and power lines down on Whitetail Dr and Dolly Varden Dr.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As an upper level low approached the Pacific Northwest coast, a mid-level jet maximum of 40 to 50 knots triggered a line of thunderstorms that moved from eastern Oregon into the Idaho Panhandle.  As this line continued to track east-northeast, they formed a gust front triggering even stronger storms over western Montana that brought strong gusty winds, frequent lightning, heavy rain and damage. The lightning detection network reported over 1500 strikes during the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter northwest of Kila reported estimated penny sized hail and strong winds estimated at 50 mph that blew off patio furniture from their deck.

A potent mid-level cut-off low moved from southwest Idaho to southern Lemhi county, Idaho by 6 am MDT Thursday October 3rd and then tracked southeast into southeast Idaho by Thursday night. The storm intensified even more as it ingested Gulf of Mexico moisture over Wyoming, allowing for wrap-around moisture to make it as far north as Butte, MT Thursday night. The storm brought snow, blowing snow and slick road conditions to portions of southwest Montana in the higher elevations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS weather sensor at the Bert Mooney Airport (5,545 ft.), located 6 miles south-southeast of Butte, Montana reported rain that changed to snow during the early morning hours of Oct. 3rd. It reported moderate snow from 9 am to 12 pm MDT on the 3rd with winds gusting out of the northerly direction between 17 and 31 mph. One to two inches of total snow fell at the airport and in the city of Butte. The Montana DOT reported snow and slush at Homestake Pass on I-90. A 60-year-old woman and her 37-year-old-son from Washington State were killed in a roll-over accident on I-90 west of Butte (west of the Ramsay exit) at 6:45 am MDT on Friday Oct. 4th. A Montana Highway Patrol trooper reported the driver was going too fast for the slick road conditions.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced a damaging microburst in the Acton area. The winds were estimated to be 90 mph with the worst damage in an area about 250 yards wide and a mile long. Structural damage consisted of a dozen mobile homes experiencing damage, two of which experienced significant damage with roof tops ripped off of them. In addition, three garages were destroyed and numerous other structures damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low dropped out of British Columbia on Sunday, October 27th and sank south to Nevada by the morning of the 28th. This allowed an arctic air-mass to spill over the Continental Divide causing strong down-slope winds to occur on the west side of the Mission Mountains. As a result, multiple reports of trees were reported on the east and southeastern side of Flathead Lake. Near blizzard conditions across Silver Bow County caused numerous automobile accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter 8 miles south of Woods Bay reported several fallen trees, one of which totaled their carport and another broke a utility light. A neighbor reported a fallen tree that damaged the metal roof of a tractor barn. The wind gust was recorded by a Heath anemometer and the spotter's location does have some tree coverage.

The lightning detection network reported over 1500 strikes during the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported nickel size hail southwest of Columbia Falls.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As an upper level low approached the Pacific Northwest coast, a mid-level jet maximum of 40 to 50 knots triggered a line of thunderstorms that moved from eastern Oregon into the Idaho Panhandle.  As this line continued to track east-northeast, they formed a gust front triggering even stronger storms over western Montana that brought strong gusty winds, frequent lightning, heavy rain and damage. The lightning detection network reported over 1500 strikes during the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Daily Inter Lake newspaper reported that a tree had fallen on a home on Kokanee Bend two miles southwest of Columbia Falls. The law enforcement scanner traffic indicated that the tree had cut the home in half trapping the people inside. They were uninjured and were eventually able to get out of the house.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A slow moving thunderstorm produced over an inch of rain in less than one hour during the evening of August 9, 2013 over the terrain southeast of Drummond, Montana. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A slow moving wet thunderstorm dumped at least 1 of rainfall in one hour just southeast of Drummond. A rancher experienced a 5 foot wall of fast moving water that moved down their driveway. The fast flowing water laden with pea-sized hail and mud severely damaged grain & alfalfa crops, irrigation sprinklers and a fence. They said it was the worst flooding in at least 20 years.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Based on photos from emergency management and in discussion with those in the area, the wind speed is estimated to be at least 90 mph.  Trees were stripped of their leaves, windows were broken, There was a home with part of the roof torn off, windows broken a shed completely destroyed, and a nearly one hundred year old barn that had been restored in the past two years that was destroyed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As an upper level low approached the Pacific Northwest coast, a mid-level jet maximum of 40 to 50 knots triggered a line of thunderstorms that moved from eastern Oregon into the Idaho Panhandle.  As this line continued to track east-northeast, they formed a gust front triggering even stronger storms over western Montana that brought strong gusty winds, frequent lightning, heavy rain and damage. The lightning detection network reported over 1500 strikes during the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The weather sensor at the Kalispell International airport reported a gust to 55 mph,  and heavy rain with reduced visibilities as low as one mile for at least 19 minutes. It reported 0.27 of rainfall in 23 minutes from 4:32 to 4:55 pm MDT. As the gust front moved through the Kalispell area, there were reports of a dust storm that brought visibilities down to near zero for several minutes before the rain began. The dust cloud moved through an open door of the Glacier High School setting off fire alarms and filling the school with dust. The school's custodial staff had to spend the night removing and cleaning the dust out. The strong wind caused large trees to fall, knocking down power lines and blocking roads. The power company reported approximately 2,500 people without power for a time that evening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong early Fall storm system moved into the Northern Rockies bringing significant snow to portions of the Billings Forecast Area. Heavy snow fell mainly along and south of a Livingston-Billings-Miles City-Baker line. The heaviest snowfall occurred along the eastern foothills of the Beartooth/Absaroka Mountains where up to two feet of snow fell. The main impacts from this storm were significant tree damage along with power outages as deciduous trees were still holding their leaves and northeast winds gusting over 20 mph added additional stress to trees and power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of 6 to 12 inches, along with tree damage and power outages, were reported in the Joliet and Bridger areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low dropped out of British Columbia on Sunday, October 27th and sank south to Nevada by the morning of the 28th. This allowed an arctic air-mass to spill over the Continental Divide causing strong down-slope winds to occur on the west side of the Mission Mountains. As a result, multiple reports of trees were reported on the east and southeastern side of Flathead Lake. Near blizzard conditions across Silver Bow County caused numerous automobile accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Montana road report for Marias Pass overnight showed snow, blowing and drifting snow. The DOT road crew estimated 3 to 4 inches at Marias Pass, although probably difficult to discern due to blowing snow. The nearby Pike Creek Snotel reported a total of 11 of snowfall with 0.30 of precipitation. The Marias Pass HADS sensor had reported 0.28 of precipitation. The Fielding RAWS station between Essex and Marias Pass reported easterly wind gusts between 20 and 33 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low dropped out of British Columbia on Sunday, October 27th and sank south to Nevada by the morning of the 28th. This allowed an arctic air-mass to spill over the Continental Divide causing strong down-slope winds to occur on the west side of the Mission Mountains. As a result, multiple reports of trees were reported on the east and southeastern side of Flathead Lake. Near blizzard conditions across Silver Bow County caused numerous automobile accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Flathead Lake Biological Station(FLBS) Mid-Lake buoy, located 8 miles east-southeast of Rollins, reported a peak wind of 65 mph from an easterly direction. A spokesperson from the FLBS reported that between 15 to 20 trees, consisting of both Tamarack & Fir, had fallen due to the winds. One of the trees fell onto a garage causing minor damage. The winds continued to gust over 50 mph through 7 am MDT on Oct. 28th. The FLBS Narrows buoy reported a peak wind gust to 50 mph at 7:33 pm MDT on Oct 27th from the northeast. According to the Mission Valley Power Company, more than 500 residences lost electrical service mostly along the eastern shore of Flathead Lake, with 350 of them located on Finley Point.  A 300-year-old ponderosa pine and root system crashed through electrical lines on the east side of North Finley Point Road at 7 pm MDT on Oct. 27th. A resident on the west side of Skidoo Bay reported 6 foot waves due to the persistent 40+ mph easterly winds. They also reported that they lost electricity for 14 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad area of diffluence over the Idaho Panhandle and northern portions of Central Montana Sunday morning brought a favorable area of synoptic lift to Southwest and Central Montana. Precipitation lagged behind the frontal passage but the most of the region was saturated and seeing snow by Sunday afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow measuring 9.0 inches in 7 hours was reported at the Dupuyer Creek SNOTEL. Elevation 5750 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major high wind event impacted most of Central Montana as the surface pressure gradient tightened across the region ahead of an approaching low pressure system. Mid level winds became quite strong (60 to 70 knots) during the afternoon, eventually mixing down to the surface at many locations over the plains and Southwest Montana valleys. The main system moved over the state Sunday with winds increasing ahead of the main Pacific frontal boundary. A second, stronger wave aloft pushed through the region on Monday before high winds began to subside.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds measuring 53KT (61MPH) were reported at the Norris Hill DOT site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first big fall storm moved through the region bringing widespread precipitation and heavy mountain snow. Strong winds developed over the Rocky Mountain Front and in the Cut Bank area as well. The first wave of precipitation moved into the region Tuesday afternoon becoming steadier by Tuesday evening. A second wave of moisture moved into Southwest Montana on Wednesday morning. Snow levels dropped to valley floors in many Southwest Montana locations. Heavy snow was seen in the mountains, especially above 6000 feet. Southwest flow aloft and a moist, unstable airmass over Southwest Montana resulted in somewhat isolated afternoon and evening convective development that produced some strong outflow winds as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow measuring 9.0 inches in 12 hours reported at the Rocker Peak SNOTEL. Elevation 8000 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system off the southern Oregon coast Thursday moved slightly inland Friday remaining nearly stationary after that. Several disturbances in the flow affected portions of Central and Southwest Montana. Strong lift over northwest portions of Central Montana during the evening, combined with a fairly unstable airmass and strong wind shear, aided in development of thunderstorms that included a threat of gusty winds, small hail, and brief heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail measuring 1.00 inches (quarter sized) and brief heavy rain were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm that originated in the Lewistown area, having a history of hail and damaging winds, resulted in a microburst by the time it reached Ingomar. A damage assessment indicated enhanced microburst winds upwards of 75 mph. Damage was a quarter mile wide and a mile in length. Numerous structures were impacted with some damage. A corner roof on one house was torn off, a metal shed was destroyed and an RV was rolled.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southwest flow aloft ahead of an incoming front brought moisture and instability to Central Montana. Airmass instability caused scattered thunderstorms to develop in the early morning hours. Lift increased later in the day and low level winds from the east to southeast brought higher dew points to much of the area. CAPE values exceeded 2200 J/kg across Central Montana by early afternoon and bulk shear numbers were also high. Additionally, wet-bulb zero heights near 9500 feet helped support large hail during the afternoon. High precipitable water values, over an inch, across a large portion of North-Central Montana supported the chances for heavy rain as well. A surface front moved across the area in the late afternoon helping keep convective activity ongoing across portions of the CWA. SPC had most of the CWA under a slight risk, the exception being Southwest Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail measuring 1.50 inches (ping pong ball sized) was reported 10 mi S of Moore, MT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first big fall storm moved through the region bringing widespread precipitation and heavy mountain snow. Strong winds developed over the Rocky Mountain Front and in the Cut Bank area as well. The first wave of precipitation moved into the region Tuesday afternoon becoming steadier by Tuesday evening. A second wave of moisture moved into Southwest Montana on Wednesday morning. Snow levels dropped to valley floors in many Southwest Montana locations. Heavy snow was seen in the mountains, especially above 6000 feet. Southwest flow aloft and a moist, unstable airmass over Southwest Montana resulted in somewhat isolated afternoon and evening convective development that produced some strong outflow winds as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow measuring 10.0 inches in 12 hours reported at the Elk Peak SNOTEL. Elevation 7600 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system off the southern Oregon coast Thursday moved slightly inland Friday remaining nearly stationary after that. Several disturbances in the flow affected portions of Central and Southwest Montana. Strong lift over northwest portions of Central Montana during the evening, combined with a fairly unstable airmass and strong wind shear, aided in development of thunderstorms that included a threat of gusty winds, small hail, and brief heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain measuring 0.82 inches in 25 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major high wind event impacted most of Central Montana as the surface pressure gradient tightened across the region ahead of an approaching low pressure system. Mid level winds became quite strong (60 to 70 knots) during the afternoon, eventually mixing down to the surface at many locations over the plains and Southwest Montana valleys. The main system moved over the state Sunday with winds increasing ahead of the main Pacific frontal boundary. A second, stronger wave aloft pushed through the region on Monday before high winds began to subside.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High sustained winds measuring 36KT (41MPH) were reported 2 mi NW of Pendroy, MT.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple power poles near the intersection of King Avenue West and Shiloh Road were knocked down due to a microburst. As a result, thousands of West End homes and businesses lost power. The thunderstorm knocked down eight poles and left about 5,200 customers and two substations without power. One of the large poles snapped about 10 feet above the ground, dragging down other power poles and light poles along the road.

The main system moved over the state Sunday with winds increasing ahead of the main Pacific frontal boundary. A second, stronger wave aloft pushed through the region on Monday before high winds began to subside.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds measuring 54KT (62MPH) were reported at the Malmstrom Air Force Base ASOS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A southwest flow aloft in conjunction with afternoon heating and an approaching short-wave trough resulted in the development of severe thunderstorms across Southeast Montana. A supercell produced softball size hail and a tornado in Powder River County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado was reported just west of Wolf Mountain and north of Post, Montana and Rough Creek. There was plenty of debris along with hail damage east of town along Highway 59. Hail accompanied the supercell thunderstorm and was reported to be golf ball to softball size.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough moved from southwest Oregon on the morning of August 25th to southern British Columbia by the morning of the 26th. This system initiated strong thunderstorms on the evening of the 25th and also during the early morning hours on the 26th. These storms produced gusty winds that caused trees to topple over causing power outages and vehicle damage across portions of northwest Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public measured strong gusty winds that pushed over three large Ponderosa Pine trees and power outages.  A tree fell onto a transmission line that impacted two power substations causing power outages for the Many Lakes and Echo Lake area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong vertical shear and moderate CAPE values combined with an upper level disturbance resulted in the development of supercell thunderstorms across the Billings Forecast Area. Large hail and damaging thunderstorm winds occurred during the afternoon and evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree branches were broken.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southwest flow aloft ahead of an incoming front brought moisture and instability to Central Montana. Airmass instability caused scattered thunderstorms to develop in the early morning hours. Lift increased later in the day and low level winds from the east to southeast brought higher dew points to much of the area. CAPE values exceeded 2200 J/kg across Central Montana by early afternoon and bulk shear numbers were also high. Additionally, wet-bulb zero heights near 9500 feet helped support large hail during the afternoon. High precipitable water values, over an inch, across a large portion of North-Central Montana supported the chances for heavy rain as well. A surface front moved across the area in the late afternoon helping keep convective activity ongoing across portions of the CWA. SPC had most of the CWA under a slight risk, the exception being Southwest Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail measuring 1.75 inches (golf ball sized) was reported in Geraldine, MT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system off the southern Oregon coast Thursday moved slightly inland Friday remaining nearly stationary after that. Several disturbances in the flow affected portions of Central and Southwest Montana. Strong lift over northwest portions of Central Montana during the evening, combined with a fairly unstable airmass and strong wind shear, aided in development of thunderstorms that included a threat of gusty winds, small hail, and brief heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds measuring 52KT (60MPH).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong afternoon heating along the high terrain combined with increased forcing from a short-wave trough resulted in the development of severe thunderstorms. A moist environment along with steep low to midlevel lapse rates and ample vertical shear supported supercells and bow structures that resulted in damaging winds and hail.||Significant crop damage occurred in Park County due to the combination of wind and hail.||The following is a Loss Assessment Report from the USDA: ||Crop;              Destroyed Acres;           Damaged Acres;      Dollar Loss||Alfalfa,             2,400,                         1,000,                 $1,764,000.00|Barley,             3,000,                            700,                 $2,268,000.00|Mixed Forage,   3,500,                          5,200,                 $1,177,500.00|Wheat,            2,000,                          1,000,                 $1,720,000.00 ||In addition, three farm structures were damaged totaling $5,500.00.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous tree limbs were broken.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid-level low pressure center moved over the Montana/Idaho/Wyoming border bringing good synoptic lift and good moisture. Wrap-around, easterly flow to the north of the low pressure center provided up-slope flow with most precipitation centered over Central and|Southwest Montana. A trailing upper level disturbance moving through western Canada resulted in the development of a surface trof, and associated high winds, along the lee side of the Northern Rockies.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow measuring 10.0 inches in 12 hours was reported at Tizer Basin SNOTEL. Elevation 6880 feet.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports from spotters, the public and emergency management indicate a very large area of significant crop loss about 5-10 miles either side of the line indicated.   Combined with some winds of up to 118 mph (estimated based on the damage photos), the hail and wind destroyed a large area of crops.  There are no good estimates of loss, but the few farmers we did speak to told us they lost thousands of acres, totaling in the millions of dollars of crop loss.

Southwest flow aloft ahead of an incoming front brought moisture and instability to Central Montana. Airmass instability caused scattered thunderstorms to develop in the early morning hours. Lift increased later in the day and low level winds from the east to southeast brought higher dew points to much of the area. CAPE values exceeded 2200 J/kg across Central Montana by early afternoon and bulk shear numbers were also high. Additionally, wet-bulb zero heights near 9500 feet helped support large hail during the afternoon. High precipitable water values, over an inch, across a large portion of North-Central Montana supported the chances for heavy rain as well. A surface front moved across the area in the late afternoon helping keep convective activity ongoing across portions of the CWA. SPC had most of the CWA under a slight risk, the exception being Southwest Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds estimated at 52KT (60MPH) were reported in Great Falls, MT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm, that originated in the Lewistown area, which had a history of damaging wind and hail, produced a couple of weak tornadoes in the Melstone and Miles City areas during the late afternoon and early evening hours of July 8th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm that originated in the Lewistown area strengthened even more by the time it reached Custer County. A bow echo structure moved rapidly through the Miles City area. A damage assessment team indicated a very strong outflow in the 75-110 mph range occurred due to a descending inflow jet that produced a short-lived spin-up tornado. This brief touchdown of an EF1 tornado was eventually outrun by the storm outflow as it moved eastward. ||Areal coverage was about one square mile along MT State Road 332 about 13 miles south of Miles City. Debris was thrown several hundred yards from the point of origin.  Several structures were impacted with parts of several roofs blown off and the side of one house blown off. Dozens of large trees 12-16 inches in diameter were uprooted and large tree limbs were thrown long distances. In addition, 12 power poles were knocked down, an irrigation pipe system was thrown several hundred feet and a camper was overturned. Other impacts included tree limbs blocking roads, power outages, campers at 12 Mile Dam (no known injuries), and major structural damage to homes, barns, and garages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong vertical shear and moderate CAPE values combined with an upper level disturbance resulted in the development of supercell thunderstorms across the Billings Forecast Area. Large hail and damaging thunderstorm winds occurred during the afternoon and evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dime size hail was also reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power outages occurred due to falling tree limbs taking out power lines, as well as a power pole being snapped.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong vertical shear and moderate CAPE values combined with an upper level disturbance resulted in the development of supercell thunderstorms across the Billings Forecast Area. Large hail and damaging thunderstorm winds occurred during the afternoon and evening hours.

Southwest flow aloft ahead of an incoming front brought moisture and instability to Central Montana. Airmass instability caused scattered thunderstorms to develop in the early morning hours. Lift increased later in the day and low level winds from the east to southeast brought higher dew points to much of the area. CAPE values exceeded 2200 J/kg across Central Montana by early afternoon and bulk shear numbers were also high. Additionally, wet-bulb zero heights near 9500 feet helped support large hail during the afternoon. High precipitable water values, over an inch, across a large portion of North-Central Montana supported the chances for heavy rain as well. A surface front moved across the area in the late afternoon helping keep convective activity ongoing across portions of the CWA. SPC had most of the CWA under a slight risk, the exception being Southwest Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail measuring 1.75 inches (golf ball sized) was reported 5 mi SW of Utica, MT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm, that originated in the Lewistown area, which had a history of damaging wind and hail, produced a couple of weak tornadoes in the Melstone and Miles City areas during the late afternoon and early evening hours of July 8th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported a brief tornado about 1 mile north of Melstone; while the town of Melstone experienced outflow winds in excess of 60 mph with minor tree and structural damage. A survey team estimated the tornadic winds to be 65 to 85 mph. No damage was reported directly from the tornado as the tornado remained over open areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of debris flows (two significant and several minor) impacted areas north and west of Corwin Springs along U.S. Highway 89. A confluence of meteorological factors combined to create a volatile situation at the southern edge of the Paradise Valley. High values of atmospheric precipitable waters combined with strong upper-level jet energy and shortwave energy moving into the region helped to create enough orographic lift to sustain a series of training thunderstorms over the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A debris flow occurred north of Corwin Springs on Highway 89, approximately 10 miles north of Gardiner. Heavy rain and the training of thunderstorms over a large catchment basin resulted in a landslide/debris flow down a designated streambed just north of Rigler Bluffs Road. Sufficient sediment and dislodged larger debris clogged a drainage culvert. The associated landslide overflowed Highway 89 and proceeded into the Yellowstone River. It was estimated that between 4000 and 6000 cubic yards of rock and sediment had to be cleared from the road and ditch to clear the culvert.  Although some minor property damage was reported, no homes were damaged as most of the debris flow remained in the established streambed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first big fall storm moved through the region bringing widespread precipitation and heavy mountain snow. Strong winds developed over the Rocky Mountain Front and in the Cut Bank area as well. The first wave of precipitation moved into the region Tuesday afternoon becoming steadier by Tuesday evening. A second wave of moisture moved into Southwest Montana on Wednesday morning. Snow levels dropped to valley floors in many Southwest Montana locations. Heavy snow was seen in the mountains, especially above 6000 feet. Southwest flow aloft and a moist, unstable airmass over Southwest Montana resulted in somewhat isolated afternoon and evening convective development that produced some strong outflow winds as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow measuring 9.0 inches in 24 hours reported at the Badger Pass SNOTEL. Elevation 6900 feet.
