EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong heating under a western states ridge combined with just enough instability to allow some strong thunderstorms to form over the higher elevations of central Montana and drift through eastern Montana. A few of these storms intensified and became severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter measured a peak wind gust of 66 mph and heavy rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system off the southern Oregon coast Thursday moved slightly inland Friday remaining nearly stationary after that. Several disturbances in the flow affected portions of Central and Southwest Montana. Strong lift over northwest portions of Central Montana during the evening, combined with a fairly unstable airmass and strong wind shear, aided in development of thunderstorms that included a threat of gusty winds, small hail, and brief heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain measuring 0.95 in one hour during the morning with 0.60 more across a couple afternoon hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong vertical shear and moderate CAPE values combined with an upper level disturbance resulted in the development of supercell thunderstorms across the Billings Forecast Area. Large hail and damaging thunderstorm winds occurred during the afternoon and evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts were estimated at 60 mph just north of Shepherd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As surface dewpoints from the southwest reached 70 degrees and daytime temperatures soared above 90 degrees, a lee-side surface trough of low pressure focused the development of thunderstorms with very strong updrafts.  One particular super-cell thunderstorm displayed textbook features and characteristics which spawned a Tornado which moved eastward across northern Roosevelt county.  Tornado damage survey conducted the following morning resulted in an EF-2 rating.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported penny-sized hail and 40 mph winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous severe thunderstorms developed during the late afternoon and early evening hours on the 19th.  The severe thunderstorms occurred to the north and west of Billings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two 5000 gallon oilfield tanks were pushed uphill from their storage area and located one mile away.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous severe thunderstorms developed during the late afternoon and early evening hours on the 19th.  The severe thunderstorms occurred to the north and west of Billings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail was dime to quarter size.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved into the Pacific Northwest coast bringing a more moist, unstable, and southwesterly flow to the region. This helped generate showers and|thunderstorms over the higher terrain of these areas. The frontal boundary associated with this system moved into western Montana later in the day bringing the chance of severe thunderstorms, damaging winds, large hail, and heavy rainfall to the area. Thunderstorm activity decreased overnight.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe hail estimated at 1.00 inches (approximately quarter sized) was reported at Goose Bay Marina 8 miles ENE of Winston, MT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple of severe thunderstorms moved across portions of Southeast Montana during the late afternoon and evening hours of the 22nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ping pong ball sized hail driven by winds estimated at 60 mph was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of instability and lift from terrain aided in development of isolated to scattered thunderstorms over a few afternoons.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds measuring 60KT (69MPH) were reported 1 mile SE of Big Sandy.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A disturbance aloft swept southeast through the plains and adjoining mountains of Central Montana. This disturbance triggered isolated to scattered thunderstorm development. Observations early in the afternoon showed a moderately unstable airmass near the Canadian border and moderate wind shear. A general threat of hail and|gusty winds with the thunderstorms resulted in SPC issuing a convective watch for much of North Central Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail measuring 1.75 inches (golf ball sized) was reported in Sunburst, MT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A high pressure ridge over the region was pushed east by a steadily advancing upper-level trough extending from Central Alberta to northern California. Small pockets of instability and moisture associated with a transient shortwave combined with generally unstable conditions to produce scattered thunderstorms along the Continental Divide and Southwest MT before spreading into Central MT during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds measured at 54KT (62MPH) were reported in Galata, MT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A southwest flow aloft in conjunction with afternoon heating and an approaching short-wave trough resulted in the development of severe thunderstorms across Southeast Montana. A supercell produced softball size hail and a tornado in Powder River County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 60 mph was estimated just north of the Tongue River Reservoir.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Weak energy and instability in a west-southwesterly flow aloft helped to generate|scattered showers and weak thunderstorms across portions of Central Montana. A few of the storms reached severe criteria.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds measuring 57KT (66MPH) were reported 27 mi N of Havre, MT. Heavy rain of .44 inches in 30 minutes was also reported with the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Weak energy and instability in a west-southwesterly flow aloft helped to generate|scattered showers and weak thunderstorms across portions of Central Montana. A few of the storms reached severe criteria.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail measuring 1.00 inches (quarter sized) was reported 12 mi ENE of Ringling, MT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 50 to 60 knot mid-level jet maximum associated with an upper level low over central Washington moved into northwest Montana by the mid-afternoon on September 6, 2013. This triggered thunderstorms that mainly brought hail and rain to the Kalispell area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Kalispell International Airport ASOS measured 0.38 of rain in 20 minutes with a west-southwest wind gust to 25 mph at 1:54 pm.

An upper level low along the California coast moved north toward Vancouver Island keeping southwest flow, moisture, and instability as fuel for isolated to scattered thunderstorms each afternoon and evening. Light winds at the surface caused storms to move quite slowly.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong vertical shear and moderate CAPE values combined with an upper level disturbance resulted in the development of supercell thunderstorms across the Billings Forecast Area. Large hail and damaging thunderstorm winds occurred during the afternoon and evening hours.
A mid-level southwest 50 knot jet maximum combined with high moisture and very warm temperatures provided the prime environment for strong supercells. These cells brought both severe wind and hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A resident reported damage to window screens and 150 fruit trees by the combination of hail and strong wind. They reported that the estimated one inch hail had a disc-shaped appearance.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper-level disturbance from the pacific northwest combined with an unstable low-level atmosphere to create many severe thunderstorms which progressed eastward across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported a few quarter-sized hail stones among the heavy rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A corridor of warm, moist air at the surface combined with an upper level disturbance and a stationary frontal boundary to produce severe thunderstorms across northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported penny-sized hail and measured 1.5 inches of rain.
